title: Advanced Machine Learning Based Detection of Islanding Using Scalogram and Histogram of Oriented Gradient
authors: Appurva Rajendra Kapil, Kumaresh Pal, Md. Qaiser Reza, Rajesh Bulbule, Somila Hashunao, Subhadip Goswami
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Electronics and Green Energy (ICETEG)
abstract: A major challenge driven on by the increasing integration of dispersed power sources is islanding. Islanding may cause harm to consumers and their equipment. The IEEE 1547 DG connectivity standards state that the DG must be shut down if islanding is detected within two seconds. This paper introduces a novel deep learning classification algorithm for image pattern recognition for islanding detection. The proposed method employs the Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) classification algorithm. Images are employed to derive the histogram of orientated gradient (HOG) features for identifying of both non-islanding and islanding events. The scalogram images are generated from the time series signal obtained at the point of common connection. The images are employed to derive HOG features, which are then utilised as feature vector inputs for training and testing the GRU classifiers. The scalogram image is generated utilising the rate of change of voltage parameter. The effectiveness of the GRU classifier is assessed using 10-fold cross-validation. The classification results demonstrate that islanding detection using a GRU classifier and HOG features for image pattern recognition achieved remarkable performance, with an accuracy of 97.43% and a detection time of 138 milliseconds.

title: Weighted Exponential-Product Fusion and Recursive Bayesian Update for Multi-Radar Cooperative Target Recognition
authors: Tao Han, Hui Chen, Yizhen Jia, Zhi Zheng, Wen-Qin Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: To address the issue that one-dimensional high-resolution range profiles (HRRPs) are susceptible to perspective variations and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) fluctuations under single-radar condition, we propose a cooperative recognition framework for multi-radar and multi-temporal sequences. Principal component analysis (PCA) compression and nearest-neighbor probabilistic discrimination are performed independently for each radar channel, producing per-radar posterior probability vectors for subsequent fusion. Multi-radar recognition results are first subjected to instantaneous fusion and sequentially updated using recursive Bayesian methods, enabling evidence accumulation and probability calibration. Simulations demonstrate that the recognition accuracy stably increases with time steps and tends to converge. With per-radar reference SNRs of 3, 1, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$-1$</tex-math></inline-formula>, and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$-3$</tex-math></inline-formula> dB, the Weighted Exponential Product (WEP) strategy, combined with instantaneous data fusion and recursive Bayesian sequential updates, forms a dual-fusion framework of multi-radar and multi-temporal sequences, thereby achieving leading performance, reaching a terminal accuracy of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$86.83\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> and improving over the best single-radar baseline at the initial step (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$61.50\%$</tex-math></inline-formula>) by 25.33 percentage points. With per-radar reference SNRs of 20, 18, 16, and 14 dB, various methods exhibit comparable performance, but the multi-radar scheme still maintains a robust advantage.

title: Reinforcement Learning-Based Path Selection Over Self-Generated Time-Evolving Multi-Metric Graphs in Disaster Response Networks
authors: Yekta Turk, Engin Zeydan, Kapal Dev
publish: IEEE Communications Standards Magazine
abstract: This paper presents a reinforcement learning (RL)-based decision framework for adaptive link selection in disaster-response networks operating under infrastructure-disrupted conditions, where communication links exhibit heterogeneous and time-varying characteristics. We model the local decision space at an edge as a time-evolving multi-metric graph tree and use RL to evaluate edge attributes such as delay, jitter, and link reliability. The proposed system learns experience-driven forwarding decisions for time-critical emergency operations, enabling adaptive behaviour with reduced reliance on centralized control. The RL algorithm traverses the generated graph tree, selecting edges that maximize accumulated composite rewards derived from multiple Key Performance Indicators (KPIs). Each layer of the graph represents snapshots of the same edge node with updated link metrics. Through simulations on time-expanded decision tree of varying depths and a hybrid physical-emulated testbed implementation, we show that the approach rapidly converges to high-quality temporal link-selection paths and effectively balances trade-offs across heterogeneous link types while remaining robust to dynamic changes in link conditions. At the end of the paper, we further discuss how the proposed framework conceptually aligns with emerging standardization efforts in IEEE, ETSI, 3GPP, and IETF for AI-native and zero-touch networking.

title: Bremsstrahlung Emission in Graphene Nanoribbon for THz Generation
authors: Luca Pierantoni, Tullio Rozzi, Davide Mencarelli, Gian Marco Zampa
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive theoretical framework for bremsstrahlung emission in graphene nanoribbons under static electric and magnetic bias fields. Charge carrier dynamics are modeled using the Dirac equation, derived from the standard graphene low-energy Hamiltonian, capturing the relativistic-like behavior of massless Dirac fermions and their interaction with electromagnetic fields. A transmission-line analogy is employed to derive analytical solutions under slowly-varying approximations, enabling insight into the emission mechanism. The radiation arises from quantum-mechanical interband transitions facilitated by magnetic-field-induced spinor mixing, rather than classical deceleration radiation. The model predicts tunable terahertz radiation in the 1–20 THz range, with emission current densities on the order of μA/m per nanoribbon, controlled by external bias and geometric confinement. Furthermore, the analysis quantifies the energy conversion performance of the system, identifying a power conversion efficiency on the order of 10−5 to 10−7. While the single-nanoribbon output power remains far below competitive levels, the framework provides a self-consistent analytical tool whose potential must be validated through array optimization and experimental verification.

title: Buffer Management for Timely Reconstruction: Lower Bounds and Near-Optimal Policies
authors: Sunjung Kang, Vishrant Tripathi, Changhee Joo, Christopher G. Brinton
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: Remote tracking systems play a critical role in applications such as IoT sensing, monitoring, surveillance, and healthcare, where updates must be sufficiently fresh for real-time decisions while also informative enough to reconstruct past trajectories. We study this dual objective for a single-source system whose state follows a Wiener process and whose updates are generated at rate <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lambda$</tex-math></inline-formula> and transmitted over a non-preemptive <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$M/M/1/2$</tex-math></inline-formula> communication link, i.e., a single-server link with exponential service times of rate <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu$</tex-math></inline-formula> and a buffer that can store at most one waiting packet. Freshness is measured using the average peak Age of Information (AoI) and the time-average AoI, while historical fidelity is captured by a long-run reconstruction error (RE) derived from LMMSE interpolation. We first analyze a stationary randomized replacement (SRP) rule that, upon an arrival to a full system, keeps the fresh packet with probability <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula>, and derive closed-form expressions for AoI and RE showing that AoI decreases monotonically with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula> while RE varies non-monotonically with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula>. In the heavy-traffic limit (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lambda \to \infty$</tex-math></inline-formula>), RE converges to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1/(3\mu)$</tex-math></inline-formula> regardless of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula>, revealing a fundamental lower bound on reconstruction accuracy imposed by the service rate of the communication link. To further navigate the timeliness-fidelity trade-off, we introduce a threshold-based dropping policy that switches from keeping old to fresh packets after a tunable threshold <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\gamma$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Closed-form analysis and numerical experiments reveal regimes where thresholding reduces RE relative to keep-fresh with only a modest age penalty, providing practical guidelines for selecting <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula> and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\gamma$</tex-math></inline-formula> across different load conditions.

title: Event-Triggered Optimal Hybrid Control for Switched Nonlinear Multiagent Systems
authors: Qiancheng Wang, Zhengrong Xiang
publish: IEEE Systems Journal
abstract: This article proposes an event-triggered optimal hybrid control policy to address the optimal switching and optimal consensus control problem for switched nonlinear multiagent systems. A novel performance index function is introduced to quantify the local neighborhood consensus error cost and control cost while capturing the switching characteristics of agents. Subsequently, the corresponding coupled switched Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation is derived. To obtain the optimal hybrid control policy, a joint optimization framework using an event-triggered adaptive dynamic programming algorithm is developed. Under the proposed event-triggered mechanism, each agent monitors its event-triggering condition. Once the condition is satisfied, the agent asynchronously updates its control input and determines which subsystem to activate, which reduces the computation and communication loads. It is proven that the closed-loop system is stable and reaches a Nash equilibrium. Moreover, Zeno behavior is proven to be ruled out. Finally, simulation results are provided to validate the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed event-triggered optimal hybrid control policy.

title: A Dynamic Spatiotemporal Hybrid Attention Model for Metro Passenger Flow Prediction
authors: Demao Zhao, Xingyu Guo, Hua Wang, Wei Chen, Mingliang Xu
publish: IEEE Intelligent Transportation Systems Magazine
abstract: Urban metro passenger flow exhibits complex spatiotemporal dependencies and pronounced nonstationarity, driven by fine-grained interstation interactions, long-range cross-line couplings, multiscale periodicity, and holiday-induced perturbations. Existing methods often rely on predefined topological structures or static propagation mechanisms, making it difficult to capture spatial correlations that vary across time and operating conditions. To address this limitation, this article proposes a dynamic spatiotemporal hybrid attention (DST-HA) model for metro passenger flow prediction. Along the spatial dimension, DST-HA develops a topology-agnostic interaction-context dual-branch hybrid attention module in which a node-level gating mechanism adaptively reconstructs internode attention weights from the current passenger flow state and fuses node-level interactions with network-level context. This design enables the learning of time-varying spatial dependencies without requiring any prior adjacency matrix. Along the temporal dimension, DST-HA introduces a context-aware spatiotemporal recurrent unit (CA-STRUs) that injects time-of-day and weekday/holiday information into gating and attention computations through linear transformations, thereby enabling adaptive memory updates under varying temporal patterns. Comprehensive experiments on real-world metro passenger flow datasets from Hangzhou and Shanghai show that DST-HA consistently outperforms strong representative baselines across multiple operating scenarios, especially under high-pressure conditions, demonstrating its practical value for congestion warning, capacity adjustment, and real-time metro operations.

title: Digital Twin Integrated Safe Deep Reinforcement Learning for Resilience Enhancement of Unbalanced Distribution Grids
authors: Fei Liu, Qianwen Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Systems
abstract: The growing integration of distributed energy resources (DERs) into distribution grids and increasing extreme events bring security challenges into grids, while accurate grid model is difficult to be obtained due to the widespread deployment of DERs and unknown extreme events. This paper proposes a new method to strengthen the resilience of unbalanced distribution networks through the integration of a novel power system digital twin (DT) and deep reinforcement learning (DRL) based multi-step critical load restoration method. First, a combined data driven and model based identification method is proposed to develop power system DT, which achieves the identification of network topology and line characteristics of distribution grids even under unbalanced conditions. Moreover, a safe DRL based multi-step critical load restoration scheme with dynamic microgrids (MGs) formation capability is established to improve distribution system resilience after extreme events and realize zero voltage violations. Finally, a DT integrated DRL based resilience enhancement approach is proposed to achieve real-time model updating, minimal load loss and guaranteed grid safety under unbalanced and uncertain grid conditions.

title: Combined Design of Targeted Rotor-Step Skewing Schemes for Modulated Vibration Mitigation in Interior Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors
authors: Daolu Li, Shengquan Li, Fang Zhang, Lijing Wang, Zihao Bian
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: This article presents a combined design of targeted rotor-step skewing schemes aimed at effectively mitigating modulated vibration induced by high-order radial force in interior permanent magnet synchronous motors (IPMSMs). Initially, the electromagnetic vibration behavior of a 36-slot/6-pole IPMSM is examined, and the dominant vibration at a specific frequency is identified. Subsequently, analytical models for vibration sources, including radial force and bending moment, are established, and their dependence on rotor circumferential position is analyzed. Then, the rotor is divided into a constant number of axial segments, and step-skewed configurations are applied. By employing various combinations of skewing mode and skewing angle, simultaneous minimization of dominant radial force and bending moment is achieved. Consequently, the optimal skewing angle is determined for each skewing mode, directly specifying the targeted rotor-step skewing schemes. Among these schemes, two types with optimal vibration performance and lower torque loss are selected. Given that they share the same minimum circumferential angle difference among all segments, a novel combined design approach is proposed. Finite element (FE) calculations demonstrate that the combined model achieves superior vibration suppression in comparison with both individual targeted designs and other combined configurations. Experimental results further validate the effectiveness of the proposed method in significantly reducing modulated vibration in IPMSMs.

title: Capacities of Entanglement Distribution From a Central Source
authors: Xinan Chen, Stefano Chessa, Ian George, Felix Leditzky, Eric Chitambar
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Theory
abstract: Distribution of entanglement is an essential task in quantum information processing and the realization of quantum networks. In our work, we theoretically investigate the scenario where a central source prepares an N-partite entangled state and transmits each entangled subsystem to one of N receivers through noisy quantum channels. The receivers are then able to perform local operations assisted by unlimited classical communication to distill target entangled states from the noisy channel output. In this operational context, we define the EPR distribution capacity and the GHZ distribution capacity of a quantum channel as the largest rates at which Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen (EPR) states and Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ) states can be faithfully distributed through the channel, respectively. We establish lower and upper bounds on the EPR distribution capacity by connecting it with the task of assisted entanglement distillation. We also construct an explicit protocol consisting of a combination of a quantum communication code and a classical-post-processing-assisted entanglement generation code, which yields a simple achievable lower bound for generic channels. As applications of these results, we give an exact expression for the EPR distribution capacity over two erasure channels and bounds on the EPR distribution capacity over two generalized amplitude damping channels. We also bound the GHZ distribution capacity, which results in an exact characterization of the GHZ distribution capacity when the most noisy channel is a dephasing channel.

title: Grant-Free NOMA in Cell-Free Massive MIMO Over Universal Filtered Multicarrier for mURLLC
authors: Changju Chen, Jiamin Li, Dongming Wang, Pengcheng Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: This paper investigates grant-free non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) in cell-free massive multiple-input multiple-output networks over universal filtered multicarrier (UFMC) for massive ultra-reliable low-latency communication (mURLLC). We identify that the even-component of the frequency domain (FD) received signal is free from both inter-sub-band and inter-sub-carrier interference, whereas the odd-component is affected by both. Initially, a covariance-based algorithm is applied for active user detection (AUD) using only the even-component. To further enhance AUD performance, we propose an innovative method that leverages the block sparsity of user activity. Additionally, we introduce an efficient method to utilize the odd-component of the FD received signal to refine the unreliable estimates obtained from the covariance-based approach. Simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed methods.

title: Stress Assessment of Bridge Steel Based on Magnetic Memory Effect Measurement and Force-Magnetic Coupling
authors: Ruize Deng, Changsheng Huang, Sanqing Su, Wei Wang, Lang Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Given the critical role of steel bridges in transportation infrastructure, conducting nondestructive testing is essential for ensuring their operational safety. The metal magnetic memory (MMM) technique provides an effective method for assessing stress in steel bridges. To address this, MMM testing was conducted on Q345qD bridge steel specimens subjected to tensile loading-unloading stress. Magnetic signals were acquired and we achieved the stress evaluation considering detection orientation using the average value Ay of the tangential magnetic field component Hmy and the linear fitting slope Kz of the normal component Hmz, yielding a maximum error of 6.68%. We further proposed a force-magnetic coupled finite element analysis approach applicable to evaluate the stress in bridge steel, which can account for the loading history. The initial boundary conditions for the finite element analysis of the magnetic field were determined based on experimental data, specifically defining the magnetic scalar potential and the component of the effective external magnetic field co-axial with the applied stress. It is expected to enhance the reliability of MMM technology for stress assessment in bridge steel.

title: A Secure and Reliable Privacy-Preserving Authentication Protocol for UAV–UAV Communications in IoT Systems
authors: Mohd Tajammul, Mohd Shariq, Gopal Singh Rawat, Sanjeev Kumar Dwivedi, Mehedi Masud, Norziana Jamil
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: The technology of Autonomous Vehicles (AVs) and Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) has evolved and contributed to improving road safety and traffic efficiency in Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS). ITS applications are dependent on a communication network formed between AVs and UAVs suffer from security and privacy issues owing to vulnerable wireless communication channels. Considering these issues, SP<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>AP, a Physically Unclonable Function (PUF) and Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)-based efficient and reliable privacy-preserving authentication protocol for secure UAV-UAV communications in IoT systems is presented in this paper. The proposed protocol not only offers anonymity and untraceability features but also mitigates UAV capture attacks. A robust informal security analysis confirms that the SP<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>AP protocol safeguards against various known security attacks, such as masquerade, Man-In- The-Middle (MITM), Ephemeral Secret Leakage (ESL), replay, node tampering, cloning, etc. The proposed protocol offers other security features, including mutual authentication, secure key agreement, no clock synchronization, user anonymity, untraceability, (for/back)ward secrecy, property, and drone-to-drone direct authentication. The formal security proof is performed using the Real-OR-Random (ROR) model, and the Scyther tool also demonstrates that the SP<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>AP protocol is more robust in terms of security and privacy. Performance analysis evaluates computation and communication costs, which show better superiority compared to similar existing protocols.

title: A Generalized Design Methodology for Multi-Input Collaborative-Flip Synchronized Switch Harvesting on Capacitors: From Theory to Optimization Strategy
authors: Jing Wang, Yi Yang, Jun Zhao, Zhen Li, Yixin Zhang, Kemeng Yang, Jiafei Yao, XiaoYang Zeng, Yufeng Guo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: This article presents a systematic design methodology for multi-input piezoelectric energy harvesting (PEH) using collaborative-flip synchronized switch harvesting on capacitors (CF-SSHCs). We address a fundamental scaling limitation: conventional synchronized switch harvesting on capacitor (SSHC) efficiency degrades as the number of parallel transducers increases due to rising equivalent capacitance. Starting from the theoretical foundations of a two-transducer single-stage topology, we develop closed-form expressions for the voltage flipping factor and power extraction that reveal a 7.4% maximum power improvement over conventional SSHC. Extending to multistage architectures, results show that an 8-stage configuration achieves 32.2% power enhancement through 33 collaborative-flipping phases. For generalized <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$M$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-input <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$N$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-stage systems, the derived analytical framework demonstrates that the maximum output power improvement ratio (MOPIR) scales from 3.8 to 9 as transducer count increases from 1 to 60 in an 8-stage implementation. We formulate an optimization algorithm for automated parameter selection and provide closed-form design equations considering practical issues, including phase asynchrony and capacitance mismatch. This work establishes a complete theoretical framework enabling systematic design of multitransducer energy harvesting interfaces rather than empirical topology exploration.

title: Robust PID Controllers with Measurement Filters for Arbitrary-order Uncertain Time-Delay Systems
authors: Elder Pereira Fenili, Leonardo Amaral Mozelli, Fernando De Oliveira Souza
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Control Systems
abstract: This paper presents a new methodology for the design of PID controllers based on Linear Matrix Inequalities (LMIs). The proposed method can be applied to arbitrary-order SISO time-delay systems. The core of the approach relies on an innovative state-space control architecture that enables the integration of arbitrary-order systems with PI/PID controllers combined with different measurement noise filters, or alternatively, with a pure PI controller. The methodology allows the consideration of multiple design objectives, including disturbance rejection, reference tracking, measurement noise attenuation, the imposition of an exponential decay rate, the guarantee of positive PID controller gains, and robustness margins in the frequency domain, by imposing upper bounds on the H∞ norm of the main sensitivity functions. Moreover, the approach explicitly accounts for parametric uncertainties. For validation, numerical examples were carried out ranging from low-order to higher-order models, combining different controller structures and multiple objectives simultaneously. The results were compared with recent and relevant methods from the literature, demonstrating the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed methodology.

title: Ubiquitous,Transparent,Security and Privacy: Challenges and Applications
authors: Nisha Rawindaran, Vinden Wylde, Pankaj Patel
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: As society advances toward Society 5.0, ubiquitous computing, IoT, and cloud-based analytics amplify the need for transparent, privacy-preserving cybersecurity governance particularly for small and medium enterprises (SMEs) that lack the resources and maturity of larger organisations. This study proposes a federated governance architecture that enables secure cross-organisational AI collaboration without centralising sensitive data. The model combines federated learning with blockchain-backed auditability to support data sovereignty, consent-aware participation, and tamper-evident accountability. A feedback-driven governance loop provides continuous monitoring and policy refinement to mitigate governance drift, while secure aggregation and proxy mediation reduce attack surfaces and regulatory exposure for SMEs. The proposed approach operationalises “compliance by design” through policy-driven enforcement, enabling SMEs to contribute to shared intelligence while retaining local control of high-risk datasets. The work outlines a viable pathway to a more secure, transparent, and socially equitable digital ecosystem aligned with Society 5.0 and emerging regulatory expectations.

title: Does Explicit Reasoning Improve Translation? Benchmarking Chain-of-Thought vs. In-Context Learning for Indonesian-English Language Pairs
authors: Tan William Marcelino Sugianto, Rendra Dwi Prasetyo, Simeon Yuda Prasetyo
publish: 2026 International Seminar on Intelligent Business and Edge-Computing Research (ISIBER)
abstract: Amidst the rapid evolution of Large Language Models (LLMs), a central debate persists regarding whether complex reasoning mechanisms, specifically Chain-of-Thought (CoT), genuinely improve translation quality for low-resource languages like Indonesian or merely inflate computational costs. Addressing this gap, this study evaluates Zero-shot, Few-shot, and CoT strategies for Indonesian-to-English translation using the Gemini 2.5 Flash model. Performance was assessed across 1,300 sentence pairs using BLEU, METEOR, and ROUGE metrics to measure success in a low-resource context. The results indicate that a Few-shot strategy using five examples with reasoning disabled offers the optimal configuration. This approach secured the highest scores across ROUGE-2 (0.4413), ROUGE-L (0.6530), and BLEU (40.68), while also consuming not so many tokens, when compared to turning on the reasoning mechanism. Conversely, activating the reasoning mechanism enhanced semantic richness but at the cost of structural accuracy, introducing unnecessary verbosity. These findings reinforce the view of translation as a pattern-matching task where providing contextual examples is more effective than enforcing stepwise reasoning. Ultimately, this work offers empirical proof that strategic prompt selection alone can significantly boost both quality and efficiency for low-resource languages without the need for fine-tuning.

title: Prediction of Javanese Language Usage Preference in Daily Life using Machine Learning
authors: Muhammad Rasyadiaz Yogatama, Rio Korio Utoro, Rikman Aherliwan Rudawan, Duddy Soegiarto, Kohbalan Moorthy, Agus Pratondo
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Blockchain, Cloud Computing, and Data Analytics (ICoABCD)
abstract: In an era of increasing globalization, the preservation of regional languages like Javanese presents a significant and complex challenge. This research addresses this issue by exploring the prediction of Javanese language usage preferences in daily life through sophisticated machine learning methodologies. By gathering comprehensive behavioral and demographic data via a structured questionnaire from 261 respondents, we developed a robust classification model. The dataset encompassed crucial variables such as age, place of residence, daily conversation patterns, media consumption habits, and overall language exposure. Three distinct algorithms-Random Forest, Logistic Regression, and Support Vector Machine-were trained and rigorously evaluated to determine the likelihood of individuals actively using the Javanese language in informal settings. The results demonstrated a significant advantage for the Random Forest model, which yielded exceptionally robust performance with an impressive accuracy of 94.64 %. This effectively demonstrates the high feasibility of using ensemble methods for complex sociolinguistic predictions. Critically, the analysis indicates that the most influential factor driving the continued use of Javanese is the frequency of an individual's active participation in traditional ceremonies and religious rituals. This important finding strongly suggests that deep-seated cultural practices serve as vital anchors for language maintenance, offering a datadriven foundation for strategic and effective preservation initiatives.

title: Does Bigger Mean Better? Comparative Analysis of CNNs and Biomedical Vision-Language Models in Medical Diagnosis
authors: Ran Tong, Jiaqi Liu, Tong Wang, Xin Hu, Su Liu, Lanruo Wang, Jiexi Xu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: The accurate interpretation of chest radiographs using automated methods is a critical task in medical imaging. This paper presents a comparative analysis between a supervised lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and a state-of-the-art, zero-shot medical Vision-Language Model (VLM), BiomedCLIP, across two distinct diagnostic tasks: pneumonia detection on the PneumoniaMNIST benchmark and tuberculosis detection on the Shenzhen TB dataset. Our experiments show that supervised CNNs serve as highly competitive baselines in both cases. While the default zero-shot performance of the VLM is lower, we demonstrate that its potential can be unlocked via a simple yet crucial remedy: decision threshold calibration. By optimizing the classification threshold on a validation set, the performance of BiomedCLIP is significantly boosted across both datasets. For pneumonia detection, calibration enables the zero-shot VLM to achieve a superior F1-score of 0.8841, surpassing the supervised CNN's 0.8803. For tuberculosis detection, calibration dramatically improves the F1-score from 0.4812 to 0.7684, bringing it close to the supervised baseline's 0.7834. This work highlights a key insight: proper calibration is essential for leveraging the full diagnostic power of zero-shot VLMs, enabling them to match or even outperform efficient, task-specific supervised models.

title: AI Corn Pest Detection via Leaf Damage
authors: Ousmane KHOUMA, Ndeye Issa KA, Pape Moussa DIOP
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Corn is a major global cereal whose productivity is heavily threatened by insects and mites from field cultivation to post-harvest storage. Lepidoptera are the most destructive pests worldwide, followed by Coleoptera, while leafhoppers and aphids worsen losses by transmitting diseases. Effective pest management depends on accurately identifying both pests and their natural enemies, yet most farmers can recognize leaf symptoms without determining the exact species responsible. This diagnostic gap highlights the need for intelligent tools that link crop damage to its cause. To address this, the study proposes an automated detection system using the EfficientNet-B4 deep learning architecture with transfer learning to classify different types of corn damage. Dataset analysis showed balanced classes and sufficient variability, and the model achieved high accuracy, robustness to lighting changes, and a strong balance between precision and recall. Overall, the approach shows strong potential as an innovative decision support tool for improving corn pest management.

title: Reducing Accessibility Gap to Specialized M-LLMs with General-Purpose M-LLMs
authors: Azeez Idris, Seok Hwan Song, Azher Ahmed Efat, Wallapak Tavanapong
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Specialized Multimodal Large Language Models (M-LLMs) are powerful, yet that very specialization creates an accessibility gap: few hospitals, labs, or small companies can afford the proprietary data, compute, and engineering needed to train or license those bespoke models. This paper introduces BIRD - Binary Inference & Resolution for Decisions, a framework that enables general-purpose M-LLMs to perform complex, specialized tasks by breaking them down into simpler binary classifications. BIRD “pecks” through the label space by posing multiple one-vs-rest questions and then fusing the binary answers into a single prediction-no additional weights, fine-tuning, or domain-specific infrastructure required. Using medical image classification as a case study, our BIRD method improved accuracy by up to 8 % on the PathMNIST dataset and an average of 5 % across four other medical datasets. BIRD provides a practical way to bridge the accessibility gap, allowing resource-constrained institutions to leverage widely available and generalized M-LLMs for specialized domains without needing domain-specific training and models.

title: HiveVeritas: An Explainable AI and Edgeoptimized Adaptive Fusion Framework for Maize Seed Vigor Prediction
authors: Nalanagula Harini, Thatimakula Sudha
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: Sustainable crop production and food security in the world require accurate prediction of maize seed vigor. Conventional biochemical and germination tests are accurate and also destructive and labour intensive making it difficult to conduct them in large scale. With current developments in hyperspectral imaging (HSI) and multimodal deep learning, it is now possible to estimate seed vigor in a non-destructive way by integrating spectral data with environmental data. On the basis of these, the HiveVeritas framework herein proposed features a flexible, understandable, and sparse multimodal framework, specifically, designed to be used in predicting the vigor of maize seed. The model involves the use of Adaptive Attention Fusion (AAF) in dynamic modality weighting and explainable AI (XAI) features like SHAP and Grad-CAM to make transparent decisions. Also, it uses Spectral-Aware Pruning (SAPrune) and Progressive Attention Distillation (PAD) to train models effectively at the edge. Experiments with a mixed spectral-environmental maize dataset demonstrate that HiveVeritas has an accuracy of 95.6%, which is 34% higher than the state-of-the-art baselines, such as Simple Fusion, CNN-LSTM, Transformer-based MMT, and HiveFusion, and has a 41 times smaller number of parameters. Explainability analysis shows the significant spectral ranges that are associated with chlorophyll and are associated with moisture absorption, making biological interpretations more important. The results show that HiveVeritas is a good balance of accuracy, interpretability and computational efficiency and thus, it is a scalable and transparent precision agriculture solution to the real world.

title: On the Identification of Fractional Parameter-Varying Wiener Systems
authors: Soumaya Marzougui, Saïda Bedoui, Kamel Abderrahim
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: This work proposes a novel approach for the combined estimation of parameters and states of nonlinear systems in a parameter-varying framework, particularly the fractionalorder parameter-varying Wiener system. An estimation procedure is developed on the basis of the auxiliary model principle and the least-squares algorithm. The simulation results demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed method of estimation.

title: Evolution of Explainable AI in Healthcare: Toward Trustworthy and Accurate Diagnostics
authors: Ibtasam Ur Rehman, Abdulraqeb Alhammadi, Jibran K. Yousafzai
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Healthcare diagnostics, treatment personalization, and clinical decision-making are being transformed by AI. This work summarizes recent literature to assess the current state of AI in healthcare. Recent AI-driven healthcare applications are examined with respect to diagnostic accuracy and algorithmic transparency. Our analysis of recent research indicates that ensemble machine learning models and deep learning approaches achieve high accuracy, ranging from 83 % to over 99 %, across several medical domains. Explainable AI methods, such as SHAP and LIME, are used to address the black-box challenge, thereby enhancing clinical trust and acceptance. Emerging issues highlighted in this review include data privacy, algorithmic bias and regulatory frameworks. Promising research directions that balance technological innovation with ethical responsibility are suggested.

title: KSGraphLSTM-Based Dynamic Liquid Level Prediction for Oil Well Production Control Systems
authors: Shizhuo Ding, Xiangyu Li, Chunhua Yuan
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Computing (SPIC)
abstract: Accurate prediction of oil well dynamic liquid levels is crucial for optimizing crude oil production efficiency and diagnosing downhole faults. Currently, data-driven soft sensing methods based on artificial intelligence technology have become the mainstream approach for dynamic liquid level prediction. However, such models rely heavily on the quality of training data—they often overlook valuable domain knowledge and struggle to fully utilize large amounts of labeled data in practical applications. To address this, this paper proposes a knowledge-driven supervised graph long short-term memory (KSGraphLSTM) model for oil well dynamic liquid level prediction. The model combines domain knowledge (e.g., Darcy's Law, wellbore flow mechanisms) with data-driven methods: first, it constructs a graph structure through the synergy of statistical correlation analysis and domain knowledge to correct spurious or missing associations between variables in the initial data-driven graph; second, it adopts a hybrid framework of graph convolutional network (GCN), graph attention network (GAT), and long short-term memory (LSTM) to simultaneously capture the spatial correlations between variables and temporal dynamic features. Experimental results on the test set show that the KSGraphLSTM model achieves a mean squared error (MSE) of 35.3, a mean absolute error (MAE) of 25.1, and a coefficient of determination <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathrm{R}^{2})$</tex> of 0.91. It outperforms the traditional LSTM model and GCN-LSTM model in all three metrics, verifying its effectiveness in improving the prediction accuracy and robustness of dynamic liquid levels.

title: An Explainable Hybrid Deep Learning Framework for DDoS Attack Detection in IoT
authors: C. Madhusudhana Rao, Polakala Amrutha, Perimila Rithika, Sai Ganesh Kumar Keta, Pappu Navya
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: Botnet-driven Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attacks are increasingly targeting Internet-of-Things (IoT) deployments, where compromised devices generate coordinated malicious traffic that causes service disruptions. Signature-based defenses and machine learning techniques that rely only on accuracy frequently struggle to adapt to changing attack patterns, class imbalance, and operational transparency requirements. This study provides an explainable hybrid deep learning intrusion detection pipeline for IoT botnet DDoS detection based on the Kaggle dataset “DDoS Botnet Attack on IoT Devices.” The methodology allows for reproducible data acquisition via KaggleHub, robust preprocessing (handling missing and infinite values, removing non-informative identifiers, label encoding, and Min-Max normalization), stratified train/validation/test splitting, and comparative modeling using classical baselines and a hybrid deep classifier. To boost analyst trust and deployment usability, the proposed system incorporates SHAP-based explainability, which provides both global feature-importance summaries and local, per-alert explanations that justify model decisions. In comparison to a rival hybrid IDS method (IMFOHDL-ID), which reports testset averages of 98.16% accuracy, 91.30% precision, 90.91% recall, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$91.08 \% \mathrm{F} 1$</tex>-score under an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$80: 20$</tex> split, the proposed SHAP-based IDS targets comparable detection performance (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx \mathbf{9 7 - 9 8 \%}$</tex> while adding transparent, analyst-friendly interpretations. Overall, the suggested solution strikes a compromise between robust DDoS detection capacity and explainability, enabling realistic IoT gateway/edge security monitoring when both performance and interpretability are crucial.

title: A Multimodal LLM-Agent for General-Purpose Video Editing with Adaptive Quality Feedback and Co-Creative Control
authors: Harish S, Alan Charles H, Nithya K
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: The huge supply of video content creates an industry-wide necessity for user-friendly and versatile video editors. Even though they exist, the current offerings often create standstill situations, especially for an editing beginner. These software solutions can have confusing systems and we espouse the ability to fine-tune ones creative layering. Even More, video editors incorporating Multimodal Interfaces have been designed to accept Natural Language NL and sketch inputs. These editors, however, are limited by a closed box of visual editing capabilities where they do not adjust to ones editing style, and do not provide the pegged quality feedback required for professional editing. This abject lack of responsive feedback encapsulates the state of the industry and shows the need for a generational overhaul of the video editing process. This paper presents a novel approach to the video editing process optimally balanced for the predicted popularity of the product to the industry. It embeds a Large Language Model LLM to provide a 'smart' user responsive and sequentially adaptive editor. The video editing professional will have their editing styles incorporated and the editor will learn reactively. This includes, to mention a few, a still missing feedback module with real time dimensionality to provide a lack of edited videos to deliver human feedback and provide an edited video quality summary. This will close the editing feedback loop and enable the user to control the editing and provide a human feedback loop by providing a fine control to have closed editing parameters. We expect to give you full control and you to expect the best editor on the market.

title: Recent Advances and Future Trends in Robot Manufacturing: A Comprehensive Review
authors: G. Mallikharjuna Rao, Srinivas Padala, Alanka Durga Bhavani, T. Aravinda Babu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Electronics and Green Energy (ICETEG)
abstract: The objective of this article is to provide a comprehensive overview of robotics in Industry 4.0, focusing on its role in transforming modern manufacturing systems. Robotics has become indispensable for enhancing productivity, precision, and scalability across industries such as automotive, aerospace, and electronics. In sectors including electronics, automotive, and aerospace, robotics has become essential for increasing efficiency, accuracy, and scalability. The vital technology areas are highlighted in this work: additive manufacturing, digital twin technologies, AI-enabled automation, and collaborative robots (cobots). It highlights developments while also critically analyzing present safety, adaptability, and system integration issues that still restrict widespread implementation. The paper also highlights the necessity for continuous academic contributions by pointing out a worrying lack of reliable research production in this area. Future research directions are described to fill these deficiencies, including autonomous decision-making, sustainable robotic systems, and human-robot collaboration. This article adds to the expanding body of literature by fusing technological insights with an emphasis on emerging trends. Its goal is to motivate academics to create globally relevant, significant solutions in robot-assisted manufacturing.

title: Enhancing Phishing Detection Using an Ensemble Model and Hyperparameter Tuning
authors: Areen Mahmoud Alrhiba, Najah Al-Shanableh, Mazen Alzyoud, Mohamed Sahbi Benlamine, Ammar Alrefai, Ahmed Khalil
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Phishing remains a serious cybersecurity challenge, preying on human error to compromise sensitive data. This study proposes an advanced ensemble learning approach combining stacking and bagging techniques with hyperparameter tuning to enhance phishing detection accuracy. Using the benchmark [1] dataset containing 11,430 URLs with 89 features, we implemented Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO) for feature selection, reducing the feature set to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 4}$</tex> most relevant attributes. Nine machine learning algorithms were evaluated including Random Forest, SVM, Naive Bayes, KNN, CatBoost, XGBoost, Gradient Boosting, AdaBoost, and LightGBM. The stacking model stood out with 98.7 % accuracy, while bagging reached 98.3 %, surpassing previous methods. These results significantly outperform recent ensemble methods on the same dataset, demonstrating the effectiveness of our approach in distinguishing between legitimate and phishing websites with minimal false positives and false negatives.

title: AI-Driven Foreign Object Debris (FOD) Detection on Airport Runways using Faster R-CNN
authors: Yogeshwaran S, Sahastrajit R, Ancy S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: Foreign Object Debris (FOD) continues to pose a grave operational and safety risk on airport runways, with even smaller objects being capable of causing engine damage, ability to down the aircraft, and huge financial losses. Conventional methods of inspection for runways are manually patrolled and visually monitored, limiting the range due to fatigue and weighted inconsistencies regarding detection accuracy. This paper thus presents an artificial intelligence based system for FOD identification using Faster R-CNN with ResNet-50 Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) backbone. The model is trained to recognize 29 different types of debris ranging from metal parts, tools, and plastic fragments, to the confounding remains of luggage-a variety representative of different runway conditions. The system adopts a distinct standard preprocessing pipeline, a deep learning based detection module, and two interfaces of deployment: a web-based FastAPI platform for static image analysis and OpenCV-based module for real-time surveillance. The capability of the system to perform high-quality inspections for small and medium debris detection, as well as the ability to generalize to images sampled from an unseen runway, has been experimentally verified. Therefore, the system is viewed as providing a robust, scalable, and operationally viable framework for enhancing runway safety, saving time on inspections, and facilitating proactive aviation maintenance.

title: Explainable and Fairness-Aware AI for Housing Subsidy Allocation in Smart-City Governance
authors: B. Chandrakala, T. Murali Karthik, U. Nitish Kumar, T. Likith Rao, M. Vijay Kumar, V. Vijaya Chandra Rao
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: Data-driven systems to distribute scarce social-welfare resources like subsidised housing are becoming an important part of smart-city governance. Nevertheless, a large body of extant artificial intelligence (AI) methods are focused on predictive performance and inadequate to meet issues of fairness, explainability, and accountability that play vital roles in decision-making in the public sector. The loophole is dangerous, as it tends to strengthen socio-economic differences and lack of trust in automated welfare. The study suggests an explainable and non-biassed AI model to implement the use of socio-economic data provided by the American Community Survey (ACS) to provide equitable housing subsidies. The suggested methodology presents subsidy allocation as a constrained optimum allocation problem that incorporates interpretable vulnerability modelling, direct constraints of fairness across groups of demographics targeted for protection and explainability in decisions. The governance layer proposed is a human-in-the-loop to facilitate policy oversight, auditing and accountability. Through experimental analysis, it is shown that the developed framework always produces the final allocation results more balanced and policy-consistent in comparison with the traditional rule-based and fairness-free baselines. The findings show better balance in the demographics with efficient coverage using the limited budget. Further, the generated explanations are indicated to agree with the policy-relevant vulnerability factors, thereby heightening transparency, and contributing to guided governance decisions. Conclusively, the suggested solution the next generation of smart-city welfare systems responsible AI makes visible the allocation efficiency, equity, and interpretability of decisions leading to the availability of transparent, accountable, and governance-friendly social-welfare-supporting decision support.

title: A Feedback-Enabled Seating Mat for Weight Shifting Training in Paraplegic Wheelchair Users
authors: Kanokpun Samalapa, Tulaya Dissaneewate, Surapong Chatpun
publish: 2025 18th International Convention on Rehabilitation Engineering and Assistive Technology (i-CREATe)
abstract: Pressure injuries are a global health concern which affects patients' quality of life and potentially leads to life-threatening conditions. This matter predominantly impacts vulnerable groups, including spinal cord-injured patients, particularly paraplegic wheelchair users. This project aimed to develop a compensatory feedback seating mat (FSM) prototype to facilitate effective weight shifting, which is crucial for preventing pressure injuries. The prototype integrated two FlexiForce <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${ }^{\text {TM }}$</tex> A201 sensors, detachably positioned beneath the high-risk ischial tuberosities areas. An Arduino UNO board, controlled by in-house-developed software, managed the system and provided visual feedback through LED lights and a seven-segment display panel. The FSM prototype was successfully developed and tested using deadweights and healthy subjects. The feedback from the FSM prototype when used by healthy subjects achieved 94.4% (51 out of 54 trials) of expected feedback. However, this work still needs to validate with paraplegic wheelchair users.

title: Design and Implementation of a Pressure Control Simulator for Water Distribution Systems Using Adaptive Fuzzy-PID
authors: Dimas Riswandi, Febi Ariefka Septian Putra, Giga Verian Pratama, Sofyan Muhammad Ilman
publish: 2025 5th International Symposium on Materials and Electrical Engineering (ISMEE)
abstract: The global water crisis demands urban water distribution systems to maintain pressure stability and minimize leakage. Pressure imbalance within distribution networks is often a primary cause of water loss. Conventional control systems such as Proportional Integral Derivative (PID) controllers face limitations in handling the complex and nonlinear dynamics of such systems. This study develops a pressure control system based on the Adaptive Fuzzy-PID (AFPID) method, with a reference pressure of 1.5 bar. The method combines the accuracy of PID control with the flexibility of a Fuzzy Logic Controller (FLC), which dynamically adjusts the PID parameters based on pressure error and its rate of change. Experiments were conducted using a water distribution system simulator under three conditions: no disturbance, single disturbance, and multiple disturbances. The results show that the AFPID controller can reduce the steady-state error to below 8%, suppress overshoot to under 10%, and achieve faster dynamic response compared to a conventional PID controller across all operational scenarios. It is concluded that the AFPID approach significantly enhances pressure control performance and adaptability to varying system conditions, demonstrating its potential for improving the stability and efficiency of urban water distribution networks.

title: Advanced Machine Learning Methodologies for Semantic Understanding and Continuous Localization of Lane Boundaries in Driver Assistance Systems
authors: Suvidha Sail, Suma Daddimani, Bhoomika Patil, Suvidha Sail
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Electronics and Green Energy (ICETEG)
abstract: Lane detection is an important subsystem of ad- vanced driver assistance systems (ADAS) and autonomous driving, which will help minimize road accidents due to unintended lane exits. In the present work, we proposed a machine learning method based on Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for lane detection with high accuracy across different driving sce- narios. Our scheme emphasizes the detection of both spatial and temporal features to enhance the reliability of lane detection in heterogenous environments such as low light and rainy weather. We trained and tested our model on the KITTI dataset and a custom-collected dataset to analyze the adaptability and perfor- mance of the model. The system proposed was able to achieve a detection accuracy of 96% highlighting its suitability for real-time implementation in dynamic road environments. Furthermore, evaluation under varying lighting conditions showed optimal IR sensor performance in low-light scenarios, with only a minor reduction at night. The research helps toward creating safer navigation systems and bridging the experimental model and real-world driving implementation gap.

title: Quickest Change Detection of Network Structure in Forest Ising Models
authors: Frank Lin, I-Hsiang Wang
publish: 2025 Asia Pacific Workshop on Data Science and Information Theory (APWDSIT)
abstract: This paper studies the problem of quickest change detection (QCD) in the network structure of a forest Ising model. We consider streaming data where, at an unknown fixed time, the network structure represented by a forest graph undergoes abrupt changes. Unlike prior works that assume full knowledge of the pre-change distribution, only the pre-change dependency structure is known, posing a challenge for designing efficient algorithms with optimal statistical guarantees. To tackle the challenge, we propose a novel framework, the Window-Limited CuSum with Least Favorable Distribution, extending the WindowLimited CuSum proposed by Xie et al. [1] to address prechange uncertainty. Our proposed framework is applicable to a broader class of QCD problems involving uncertainty in both pre- and post-change distributions, and may be interesting in its own right. By tailoring the framework to the structural change detection problem, we develop the Chow-Liu-Like Window-Limited CuSum with Least Favorable Distribution, a computationally efficient procedure that employs a greedy edge selection strategy inspired by the Chow-Liu algorithm to reduce the complexity of searching through post-change structures. Combined with a matching information theoretic lower bound, we characterize the asymptotic fundamental limit of the problem.

title: Compact Ultrawideband Antenna for Next-Generation Brain Machine Interfaces
authors: C. Nandagopal, Indhu M, Jeevitha P, Harshavarthini R B, Infant Bena B
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: The development of compact and efficient antennas is a critical requirement for next-generation brainmachine interface (BMI) systems, which demand reliable wireless communication, low power consumption, and minimal form factors. This paper presents a compact ultrawideband (UWB) antenna designed to meet these stringent requirements for wearable, implant-proximate, and portable neurointerfacing applications. The proposed antenna occupies a compact footprint of 30 mm × 28 mm and operates over a wide frequency range, exhibiting excellent impedance matching characteristics. A minimum return loss of -28 dB is achieved at 5.8 GHz, while the voltage standing wave ratio (VSWR) remains close to unity across the operating band, indicating negligible signal reflection and efficient power transfer. The antenna demonstrates stable and nearly omnidirectional radiation patterns with high symmetry, ensuring reliable communication regardless of device orientation. Both two-dimensional and three-dimensional radiation analyses confirm uniform field distribution and consistent performance throughout the UWB spectrum. By effectively integrating wide bandwidth, compact size, and stable radiation characteristics, the proposed antenna provides a practical solution for low-power, high-precision wireless communication in neural monitoring, cognitive control systems, and biomedical telemetry. The presented design contributes toward enhancing wireless reliability and electromagnetic compatibility in modern BMI platforms.

title: The Impact of AI-Based Chatbots on Reducing Cart Abandonment in Indonesian E-Commerce
authors: Baby Amorina, Tanty Oktavia
publish: 2026 International Seminar on Intelligent Business and Edge-Computing Research (ISIBER)
abstract: The growth of e-commerce in Indonesia continues to increase rapidly, but the cart abandonment rate is still a big challenge. By 2025, around 84.8% of e-commerce users in Indonesia will leave the shopping cart without completing a transaction. One of the solutions that is widely implemented is the use of artificial intelligence (AI)-based chatbots to assist users during the purchase process. This study aims to analyze the influence of perceived usefulness, behavioral intention, and online purchasing intention on the decrease in cart abandonment on e-commerce platforms in Indonesia. Using the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) model, a quantitative approach was carried out on 400 e-commerce user respondents who had interacted with chatbots. The data was analyzed using the Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) method. The results showed that the perception of usability had a significant effect on behavioral intent, which in turn increased online purchase intent and decreased cart abandonment rate. These findings strengthen the application of TAM in the context of AI-based technology, while providing practical implications for e-commerce players to optimize the function of chatbots in increasing purchase conversions.

title: Comparative Study of BERT and Conventional NLP Models for Kannada News Categorization
authors: Nikhil Jonna, Trishitha Tirupathi, Sireesha K
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: This paper will involve a comparative evaluation of the traditional machine-learning models and transformer-based models to classify Kannada news headlines. In the article, Kannada headlines were processed and normalized, and then, they were coded with TF-IDF and trained on five classic classifiers: Logistic Regression, random forest, Decision Tree, Multinomial Naive Bayes, and Linear Support Vector Classifier (Linear SVC). Simultaneously, a BERT model that had been trained on a corpus of relevance was fine-tuned to the target corpus and tested. Empirical findings show that the Linear SVC model will have the best accuracy among the classical models, and the fine-tuned BERT model will far outperform any other traditional methods. These results indicate how transformer architectures can be used with low-resource Indian languages like Kannada.

title: Development of a Camera-Based Non-Contact Hand Rehabilitation Support System
authors: Xinyu Hu, Ariaki Higashi, Naoko Shinagawa, Yuichi Kurita
publish: 2025 18th International Convention on Rehabilitation Engineering and Assistive Technology (i-CREATe)
abstract: Stroke is a major health problem in Japan and a leading cause of long-term care, particularly among individuals aged 40-64. Many patients continue living at home while undergoing rehabilitation, but conventional devices are often expensive, bulky, and burdensome. This study proposes a camera-based, non-contact hand rehabilitation support system that tracks MCP, PIP, DIP, and IP joint movements in real time. The system incorporates Brunnstrom Recovery Stages (BRS), the Simple Test for Evaluating Hand Function (STEF), and the Fugl-Meyer Assessment (FMA) to assess patient progress and provide recovery-stage-specific training. This approach reduces patient burden and improves accessibility. Future work will enhance motion recognition accuracy and validate the system's effectiveness with clinical trials.

title: Fuzzy Logic-Based Intelligent Conveyor Motor Control Using ESP32 and PLC Outseal
authors: Muhammad Abdillah Zakka, Rizky Ajie Aprilianto
publish: 2025 5th International Symposium on Materials and Electrical Engineering (ISMEE)
abstract: Industrial conveyor systems require an intelligent control mechanism to adapt motor speed to dynamic load variations, ensuring stable performance and optimal energy efficiency. However, conventional conveyor controllers with static speed regulation often result in energy waste, slow response, and mechanical wear. This research proposes a fuzzy logic-based motor speed control system implemented on an ESP32 microcontroller and integrated with a PLC Outseal. The proposed system uses a load cell and infrared sensors to measure load weight and motor speed and adjusts the PWM signal to the BTS7960 driver based on real-time fuzzy inference. The Modbus RTU protocol integrates the system with the Node-RED Dashboard to facilitate flexible monitoring and manual control. The fuzzy controller is designed with a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3} \times \mathbf{3}$</tex> rule base, using load weight and material level as inputs, and PWM duty cycle as output. Experimental validation shows that the system reaches a steady state in less than 4.1 seconds with an error of less than 5%, and the rise time is less than 2.01 seconds at all speed levels. In addition, energy consumption is reduced by 72%, outperforming conventional systems with fixed speed. These results demonstrate the system's effectiveness in providing stable, responsive, and energyefficient motor control, especially suitable for small- to medium-scale industrial automation environments.

title: Artificial Intelligence in Slovak Higher Education: Perceptions and Patterns of Use among Students
authors: József Udvaros, Veronika Gabal’ová
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: The rapid development of artificial intelligence (AI) is significantly shaping education, bringing both opportunities and challenges for higher education institutions. This study explores how Slovak university students perceive the use of AI in the learning process, their experiences with specific tools, and their attitudes toward its formal integration into the academic environment. The research is based on a questionnaire survey addressing the frequency of AI use, the diversity of tools employed (e.g., ChatGPT, Copilot, Gemini, Bard, Meta AI), perceived benefits and concerns, and views on incorporating AI into teaching. Data were analyzed using descriptive statistics, correlation tests, and multiple regression analysis, while openended responses were examined through thematic analysis. Preliminary results indicate that frequent AI use and higher perceived benefits increase the likelihood of supporting its integration, whereas concerns and risk perceptions reduce it. The study provides empirical insights into the current level of AI adoption among Slovak students and offers recommendations for universities aiming to implement AI in an effective and ethical way.

title: 3D CNN with Multi-ROI Features for Alzheimer's Classification from Brain MRI
authors: Yahya Ayyash Ashdaqi, Riyanarto Sarno, Alya Kamilah, Dalila Anwar Dini Rasyidah, Laurensia Simanihuruk, Kelly Rossa Sungkono, Aziz Fajar, Asra Al Fauzi, Setiawan Hadi
publish: 2026 International Seminar on Intelligent Business and Edge-Computing Research (ISIBER)
abstract: As a leading neurodegenerative disorder, Alzheimer's Disease (AD) impacts millions globally and constitutes a critical public health concern. Early detection through Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) analysis is crucial for patient management and therapeutic intervention. Existing deep learning approaches either use whole-brain volumes, which are computationally expensive, or focus on single regions like the hippocampus, potentially missing important multi-regional patterns. This study proposes a Multi-Region of Interest (Multi-ROI) 3D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with Attention Mechanism for AD classification. The proposed method extracts 12 Alzheimer-relevant brain regions simultaneously using the Automated Anatomical Labeling 3, version 1 (AAL3v1) atlas and processes them as multi-channel input to a custom five-stage residual 3D CNN with multi-level Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM). The selected regions cover memory network, default mode network, and semantic processing areas. Experiments on the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) dataset demonstrate that the Multi-ROI approach achieves a mean Macro-AUC of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.916 \pm 0.031$</tex> and Macro-F1 of 0.792 in three-class classification (Cognitively Normal, Mild Cognitive Impairment, and AD) with only 4.7 million parameters, representing an 86% reduction compared to standard whole-brain 3D ResNet-18 while capturing multi-regional patterns missed by single-ROI methods.

title: A Comprehensive Taxonomy and Dynamic Optimization Framework for Fuzzy C-Means Clustering
authors: Said Iskandar Al Idrus, Mahyuddin K. M. Nasution, Ade Candra, Maya Silvi Lydia
publish: 2026 International Seminar on Intelligent Business and Edge-Computing Research (ISIBER)
abstract: Fuzzy C-Means (FCM) remains one of the most widely adopted soft-clustering algorithms across computer science applications; however, its performance is strongly influenced by fixed cluster numbers, sensitivity to initialization, and vulnerability to local optima. This study presents a comprehensive taxonomy of FCM developments, covering classical variants, optimization-driven hybrids, kernel-based models, adaptive approaches, and high-performance implementations. Building upon the taxonomic analysis, we propose a Dynamic Optimization Framework for FCM (DOF-FCM) that enables automatic cluster adaptation and improved convergence stability. The framework integrates dynamic cluster number estimation with an optimization function that refines cluster centers through an iterative search mechanism, producing more robust partitions in noisy or high-dimensional environments. Experimental evaluation demonstrates that DOF-FCM yields superior clustering accuracy and reduced objective function values compared to static FCM and other hybrid methods. This research contributes both a structured understanding of the FCM landscape and a novel dynamic model that advances adaptive fuzzy clustering for modern data-intensive applications.

title: Interpretable Machine Learning in Guided Synthesis of Stable Sols Based on Nanosized Titanium Oxides
authors: Alexander Glushko, Alexey Neznanov, Galina Kuz'micheva, Asiya Gainanova
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: This report discusses the guided synthesis of sols containing nanosized titanium(IV) oxides for use in biological and medical applications. These sols vary in size (from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 2$</tex> up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2000 \mathbf{n m}$</tex>) and different stability (from 0 up to 90 days). They are synthesized under changing fabrication conditions (temperature, hydrolysis duration, titanium-containing precursors composition and concentration) without surfactants. A specific set of machine learning tools was developed for well interpretable analysis of experimental data. These tools were used in three experimental stages to optimize the three functional characteristics of the synthesized sols changing by five synthesis parameters. The result is a, universal minimalist mechanism for guided synthesis, which allows the relationships between synthesis parameters and functional characteristics to be studied in various experiments.

title: A Cyber-Security Framework for Behavior-Driven Ransomware Detection Using Graph Neural Networks on System Call Dependency Traces
authors: Chinthalapalli Kamsali Indira, Narravula Asritha, Bhumireddy Jagadeeswar Reddy, Mala Ramcharan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: Ransomware has remained among the worst types of cyber security threats, and the contemporary types have been changing polymorphism, dynamism in delivery of payloads, and evasion mechanisms rendering the conventional signature based defenses futile. Most of the currently used detection systems rely on the static feature or linear sequence model, which is not a complete reflection of the sophisticated behavioral patterns of system call interactions. To address such constraints, this paper presents a new framework of cyber security-related behavioral ransomware detection based on Graph Neural Networks on system call dependency traces. The suggested approach builds elaborated dependency graphs that reflect on time-dependence, causal transitions and co-occurrence between system calls. These graphs do not lose the structure and behavioral features between ransomware activity and the normal functioning of systems. It has an adaptive Graph Neural Network architecture to learn higher level graph features, with a reduced impact on sparse traces, noisy traces, or on purpose obfuscated traces. The framework uses behavior scoring, augmentation of graphs, multi stage dependency fusion to enhance the resilience of the detection model to adversarial manipulation and zero day ransomware variants. Recent ransomware datasets demonstrate consistent improvements on the accuracy, early detection, and generalization compared to the traditional machine learning models and deep sequence based models in experimental evaluation. The findings validate that the representation of next generation ransomware defense is an effective and scalable platform that can be modeled with system call dependencies in the form of graph structures and GNN based learning.

title: Fire Detection System Using Deep Learning
authors: Umme Amarah, Pasalapudi Siddhi, Sameera Khan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: Fire accidents are very dangerous. It causes harm to people, buildings and their properties. Traditional Fire Detection Systems like heat sensors, smoke detectors are used in many places. These systems work by sensing smoke, heat or harmful gases. But the traditional systems have many problems like they may take more time to detect fire, cover only small area, produce false alarms. It may also fail to detect fire in open and complicated areas. To solve these type of problems Real Time fire detection system using deep learning is introduced. This system can easily and quickly detect fire using live video from normal cameras without using any special sensors or hardware. It uses computer vision and deep learning techniques to watch the video and find fire. In this project, a special deep learning model called YOLOv5 (You Only Look Once) is used because it can detect objects quickly and accurately. Dataset containing thousands of Fire and Non-Fire images are used to train the model. These images are taken in different lighting conditions, backgrounds, and fire sizes. Because of this training, the system can correctly tell the difference between real fire and things that look like fire, such as sunlight, vehicle lights, or reflections. This helps reduce false alarms.

title: Deep Learning-Based Ophthalmic Ultrasound Image Classification System for Assisted Diagnosis
authors: Bo Li, Tingxue Li, Dingnan Zhang, Mingyu Shi, Fanghui Mang, Zixuan Wen, Zhihong Lu
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Computing (SPIC)
abstract: Color Doppler ultrasound plays a crucial role in the diagnosis of ocular diseases. Up to now, the auxiliary diagnostic means based on deep learning focus on the image features of the lesion area and often ignore the text information such as blood flow velocity as well as vascular resistance index in ultrasound images. This research designed an ophthalmic ultrasound diagnostic system integrating image detection, text extraction and disease classification to solve this issue. This system adopts a multi-stage processing architecture: YOLOv11 locates pathological areas, PaddleOCR extracts hemodynamic parameters, as well as EfficientNetB1 performs classification tasks covering seven ophthalmic conditions (including normal fundus and vitreous opacity). By integrating image and text information, the system has imoroved the accuracy as well as automation of diagnosis. The experimental results indicate that the classification accuracy of this method exceeds 95%, which is superior to the existing ultrasound diagnostic systems. This research proved the fusion of visual and textual information, improving diagnostic accuracy as well as fleetly generating ophthalmic diagnostic reports, providing a solution for intelligent ultrasound-assisted diagnosis..

title: Adaptive UAV-Assisted Signal Acquisition and Low-Latency Processing Framework for Distributed Measurement Systems
authors: Irem Bayraktar, Murat Bakirci
publish: 2026 28th International Conference on Digital Signal Processing and its Applications (DSPA)
abstract: Distributed measurement infrastructures increasingly depend on low-latency signal acquisition and rapid data processing to support time-sensitive decision-making. In practice, fixed sensor deployments are often constrained by limited spatial coverage, communication bottlenecks, and performance degradation during periods of high network load.
