To address these limitations, this study introduces a UAV-assisted signal processing framework that adds an aerial processing and relay layer to improve measurement reliability, shorten transmission paths, and stabilize end-to-end signal delivery. The proposed architecture combines ground-based sensing streams with UAV-collected visual and environmental data through adaptive fusion mechanisms, enabling dynamic spatial coverage and targeted signal reinforcement in sparsely observed regions. UAV positioning is guided by signal relevance metrics and congestion indicators, allowing the system to adapt the effective acquisition topology and reduce multi-hop communication overhead. Experimental results show that the framework increases packet delivery reliability by up to 15.8%, reduces end-to-end processing latency by nearly 48% under congested network conditions, and achieves up to a 47.9% reduction in actuation-trigger latency for time-critical events.

title: Building Competences in Artificial Intelligence and Learning Analytics for Personalized Teaching in Higher Education
authors: Apostolos Kostas, Dimitris Spanos, Filippos Tzortzoglou, Georgeta Chirlesan, Athina Konstantinidou, Efi A. Nisiforou, Merike Saar, Carlos Vaz de Carvalho
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Personalization and digital transformation are now recognized as central drivers of student engagement and achievement in Higher Education, as the traditional one-size-fits-all approach increasingly fails to meet diverse learning needs. Yet, HE institutions often lack the resources, skills, and frameworks to adopt practices that would allow that transformation, namely through the adoption of Learning Analytics and Artificial Intelligence tools and methodologies. The LEADER AI initiative responded to these challenges by equipping faculty, staff, and instructional designers with practical resources, tools, and training to design and deliver personalized teaching and learning experiences. After piloting and validation across multiple countries, results demonstrated strong usability, positive feedback, and a significant potential to create inclusive, student-centered HE environments and to prepare resilient educators capable of leveraging advanced technologies to improve their teaching.

title: Reducing Hallucination in LLMs via Monte Carlo Tree Search with Knowledge Graph Guidance
authors: Dhruv Patel, Lisa Fan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) such as ChatGPT, Bard, and LLaMA have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in natural language processing tasks including summarization and question answering. However, their tendency to hallucinate - producing fluent but factually incorrect content - limits their reliability in domains such as healthcare, finance, and risk analysis. This paper presents a comparative study of hallucination mitigation strategies, applying Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) and Test-Time Sampling (TTS or Best-of-N) to the Meta's LLaMA-3.2-3B model using the HaluEval dataset. Experimental results show that MCTS improves factual accuracy by approximately 10% over the baseline LLaMA-3.2-3B model, achieving 85.6% accuracy and performing comparably to the Qwen3-14B model, which is nearly three times larger. The study is further extended with Knowledge Graph (KG) integration for biomedical question answering on PubMedQA, demonstrating that KGguided MCTS enhances factual reliability in specialized contexts. These findings highlight the potential of search-based reasoning and structured knowledge to reduce hallucination and strengthen factual consistency in generative language models.

title: 4LM: Local Lightweight LLMs for Image Captioning on Embedded Systems
authors: Ibrahim Bougacha, Mohamed Hakmouni, Mohamed Youssef Abdelhedi, Mohamed Karaa, Siwar Hammami, Adel M. Alimi, Lokman Sboui
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: Deploying Large Language Models (LLMs) on edge devices has gained attention due to strict latency, privacy, and bandwidth requirements in modern applications. However, edge resource-constrained platforms present a bottleneck for LLM deployment, particularly for vision-language applications. In this work, we propose 4LM (Local Lightweight LLM), a framework for deploying lightweight LLMs for image captioning on edge devices such as the Raspberry Pi. We apply quantization techniques to reduce the memory footprint and computational complexity of the models, enabling more efficient on-device inference. We examine the trade-off between model size and captioning quality. Results show that models such as blip2-flan-t5-xl and LLaVA-Qwen- 0.5 B retain competitive performance, highlighting their potential for deployment on edge devices.

title: Data-Driven Prediction of Lithium-Ion Battery Rul and Capacity Using Health Indicators
authors: Zouhour Souissi, Anis Messaoud
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: Accurate and reliable prediction of the remaining useful life (RUL) and capacity of lithium-ion batteries is crucial for effective battery management and predictive maintenance. In this study, three indirect Health Indicators are extracted from voltage and current profiles during charging and discharging to provide practical and measurable signals for degradation monitoring. Three advanced machine learning models: Gaussian Process Regression (GPR), Gaussian Mixture Regression (GMR), and Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), are employed to predict capacity and RUL. The GPR model is optimized using a Matern kernel and multidimensional inputs to enhance long-term prediction accuracy, GMR captures local degradation trends with probabilistic confidence intervals, and XGBoost provides highly precise RUL estimation near the end-of-life. Comparative analysis on representative batteries demonstrates that each model offers distinct advantages and reliable performance. The results indicate that using indirect HIs combined with optimized machine learning models enables accurate, robust, and interpretable RUL and capacity prediction.

title: Phase Change Materials for Enhanced Thermal Energy Storage in Building Applications
authors: Ayman Alarqan, Skandar Rejeb, Samia Ben Ali
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: Phase Change Materials (PCMs) provide a passive approach for controlling indoor temperatures through latent heat absorption and release within the thermal comfort range. A transient computational simulation was performed using ANSYS Fluent to evaluate the thermal regulation capabilities of a brick wall containing an internal PCM layer slightly encapsulated within a thin plastic shell to ensure stability and prevent leakage. The model simulates realistic daily temperature variations on the outer surface and natural convection indoors. Three wall designs were examined: one without PCM, one with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 5 ~ c m ~ P C M}$</tex>, and one with 2.5 cm PCM. Results show that integrating PCM decreases the maximum inner surface temperature by about 1.8° C and delays thermal wave propagation by up to 11.4 h in the thicker layer. Temperature fluctuation amplitude was also decreased by up to 58 %, demonstrating strong thermal damping. The liquid fraction analysis reveals cyclic phase transitions, highlighting the PCM's capacity to capture heat during peak times and discharge it afterward. This analysis confirms the potential of PCMs to increase wall thermal inertia and enhance energy efficiency under realistic climate conditions.

title: A Multi-Modal Dataset for NFT Recommendation Systems
authors: Durmuş Aydoğdu, Nizamettin Aydin
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Distributed Ledger Technologies (ICDLT)
abstract: There is a lack of standardized datasets for NFT (Non-Fungible Token) recommendation systems. This study presents a comprehensive dataset designed for NFT recommendation systems, incorporating both NFT-related data (e.g., images, textual descriptions, rarity and transaction data) and user-related data (e.g., purchase price, transaction duration, and NFT holding period). To create the dataset, a Data Collection Tool was developed to gather raw data via the OpenSea API, and a Data Preparation Tool was implemented for preprocessing and filtering. All data used in this study are publicly available and anonymized, ensuring that user privacy is fully preserved. The dataset is evaluated using NFT-NCFAE, a deep learningbased NFT recommendation model, with performance measured by Recall and NDCG evaluation metrics. The evaluation results demonstrate the suitability and value of the proposed dataset for NFT recommendation systems. By making the dataset and its associated tools publicly available, this work aims to establish a benchmark for future research and enable comparability across different models.

title: Improved Current Quality in Two-Stage Small Wind Turbine: Full-Bridge Converter and Six-Arm Inverter
authors: Barzelius Vincent Dika Wicaksana, Leonardus Heru Pratomo
publish: 2025 5th International Symposium on Materials and Electrical Engineering (ISMEE)
abstract: Utilization of renewable energy sources will be a crucial solution to the energy crisis problems. One of global alternatives is using wind energy in a small wind turbine. The implementation of this small wind turbine system required the operation to elevate voltage and convert current into sinusoidal. Conventional inverter uses a Digital Signal Processor (DSP)-based microcontroller to control the power switches. To enhance the efficiency of power conversion in this small wind turbine system, the two-stage configuration is the optimal solution. A full-bridge converter is used for the initial stage. This architecture employs a high-frequency transformer. To optimize the power conversion efficiency of the small wind turbine, a six-arm inverter is employed in the second stage, producing sine wave current with minimal total harmonic distortion (THD). The thermal load is evenly distributed on each inverter arm, thereby minimizing hot spots and improving the operational durability of the power switches. This system can be operated with the minimum Arduino Mega 2560 configuration to simplify the control system. The experiment shows that using a two-stage system in a small wind turbine power generation system can achieve PT PLN standards.

title: Effect of High Current Density Electrodeposition of Zn-Mn on Aluminum Air Battery Anode
authors: Ruly Bayu Sitanggang, Syarifa Nur'aini, Hendrix Abdul Ajiz, Tantular Nurtono, W. Widyastuti, Heru Setyawan
publish: 2025 5th International Symposium on Materials and Electrical Engineering (ISMEE)
abstract: Aluminum (Al) is identified as a highly promising material for use as a solid anode in energy storage applications. This study involves the electrodeposition of a zinc-manganese (Zn-Mn) layer onto aluminum 6061 to enhance its electrochemical properties. The investigation of the aluminum surface was conducted using techniques including X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and the electrochemical stability window (ESW). To evaluate the electrochemical performance of the modified aluminum, parameters such as capacity, charge transfer resistance (Rct), and corrosion resistance were analyzed. The results indicate that the deposited aluminum exhibits a faradaic current and demonstrates reversible current behavior with a shift towards the negative potential region in comparison to Al 6061. The measured specific capacity and energy density of the aluminum are <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$312.5 \text{mAh}. \mathrm{g}^{-1}$</tex> and 164.15 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{m W h}. g^{-1}$</tex> per gram, respectively. These results underscore the enhanced electrochemical properties of the deposited aluminum, suggesting its potential for future use in Al-air batteries.

title: Multiple Influences on the Future of Agricultural Product Drying in Agriculture: Regional Perspectives
authors: Hocine Bensaha, Abdelouahab Benseddik, Djamel Daoud, Sofiane Kherrour, Ali Bensaha, Hamza Ammisaid, Lamine Cheraa, Ghania Hamdoune
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: This article examines the evolution of agricultural drying under the influence of a variety of interdependent factors. These include the dynamics of national food chains, societal controversies over agricultural models, the recognition of drying as a profession, and advances in rural development. We also analyze the issues related to the innovation and diffusion of this technology. By synthesizing these influences and their implications, we highlight the key parameters for anticipating future trajectories of agricultural drying.

title: Novel Approach to Achieve Multi-Dimensional View of DevOps Adoption in Tech Culture: From Legacy to Agility
authors: Dileep M R, Harshavardhan R K, Vidya Sagar S D
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Electronics and Green Energy (ICETEG)
abstract: This study introduces a practical framework that looks at DevOps adoption from multiple angles focusing on cultural shifts, process improvements, and automation. By combining surveys and real-world case studies, the research explores the main challenges organizations face and the factors that contribute to successful DevOps implementations. The data, gathered from over 60 professionals across various industries, reveals significant improvements in how often teams deploy software, the speed of delivery, and collaboration between development and operations. Our proposed Goal-Question-Metric (GQM) framework offers companies a clear guide to move from traditional legacy systems toward agile, cloud-native environments. The insights gained from this study can help organizations grow their DevOps maturity, promote stronger cross-team cooperation, and align their software delivery more closely with business goals-ultimately leading to faster, more reliable software releases.

title: Self-Steering Reduced-Complexity Adaptive Antenna Array for Global Navigation Satellite Systems
authors: Victor I. Djigan
publish: 2026 28th International Conference on Digital Signal Processing and its Applications (DSPA)
abstract: This paper presents an Adaptive Antenna Array (AAA) for usage in ground-based receivers of Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS). This AAA allows to cancel the received interfering signals at its output. Typically, the Side Lobe Cancellers (SLC) or the Linearly Constrained (LC) AAAs are used for this purpose. However, the SLC AAA does not ensure any considerable values of the Radiation Pattern (RP) towards the informational signal sources (satellites) and the LC AAA guarantees these values only in the vicinity of the direction of a linear constraint of the RP. As a result, some satellite signals might not be received even when the interfering signals are suppressed. Another type of AAA is the so-called correlated multibeam AAA. It is able to steer its beams towards navigation satellites simultaneously with the suppression of the interfering signals. However, independently of the type of the used adaptive algorithm, this AAA is a computationally complex because of its multibeam architecture and usage the sets of own correlators or matched filters for each beam. This complexity can be reduced about two times in adaptive algorithms part if the AAA has odd symmetry. This reduction is achieved due to the ability of such AAA to execute almost all of its computations in real-valued arithmetic. In odd-symmetric AAA, the received signal samples and the weights in the channel pairs, whose antennas are symmetric with respect to the AAA phase center, are complex conjugated. An odd-symmetric correlated AAA that uses the Recursive Least Squares algorithm for the weight calculation is considered in this paper. A single-beam AAA and its computational procedure are presented. Simulation results demonstrate the AAA performance with about two times shorter transient response and about 3 dB bigger steady-state suppression of interfering signals, compared to the same complex-valued arithmetic AAA.

title: From Fragmentation to Trust: Standardizing Communication in Federated Learning
authors: Sridharan Sankaran
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative training of machine learning models without sharing raw data, offering a pathway to privacy-preserving innovation in sensitive domains such as healthcare, finance, and telecommunications. Despite its promise, the FL ecosystem remains fragmented: frameworks like TensorFlow Federated, Flower, and PySyft each define proprietary communication protocols, creating “walled gardens” that hinder interoperability, inflate engineering costs, and restrict participation across heterogeneous infrastructures. This paper argues that standardization of FL communication is essential not only for technical efficiency but also for fostering systemic trust. We propose a Federated Learning Communication and Interoperability Protocol (FL-CIP) as a unifying layer that defines baseline communication primitives for client–server federations—such as handshake, task messaging, model serialization, update reporting, and error codes—while extending to peer-to-peer scenarios with neighbor discovery, model update exchange, and topology synchronization. Through this lens, interoperability becomes the foundation of trust, transparency and auditability strengthen accountability, and open standards prevent vendor lock-in, enabling fair participation. Challenges remain in embedding security, designing governance models, and balancing extensibility with stability, but lessons from past standardization domains underscore the feasibility of this effort. FL-CIP thus provides a blueprint for transforming fragmented frameworks into a trustworthy, inclusive, and scalable federated learning ecosystem.

title: Implementation of a Cost-Effective Arduino UNO Based Drone for Agricultural Applications
authors: Gayitri H M, K P Bhoomika, Sanjana B K, Keshav Skanda Sai, Mallikarjun Kuragod, Halesh M R
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Electronics and Green Energy (ICETEG)
abstract: In this paper, a low-cost quadcopter for smallscale agricultural applications based on the Arduino Uno platform is designed and implemented. For stable manual flight, the system combines necessary parts like brushless DC motors, electronic speed controllers (ESCs), an MPU-6050 IMU, and a FlySky FS-i6X transmitter-receiver module. For orientation stabilization, a proportional-integral-derivative (PID) control algorithm is used, guaranteeing dependable balance and maneuverability. An 11.1 V LiPo battery was used to power the prototype, which was built on a lightweight F450 frame. The experimental evaluation revealed a maximum payload capacity of roughly 250 g, responsive directional control, and stable hovering. The drone's average flight time was 10 minutes, and it had a horizontal coverage range of roughly 100 meters. The results validate the viability of employing inexpensive, opensource hardware for realistic agricultural operations, despite payload capacity and flight endurance limitations. The suggested platform lays the groundwork for upcoming enhancements that will lead to more reasonably priced precision farming solutions, such as GPS-based autonomy, larger-capacity batteries, and sensor integration for crop monitoring and precision spraying.

title: Hair Artifact Removal in Dermoscopic Images using DWT and VAE-GAN
authors: Suresh M, Sudarsan M, Shirly Ritika C, Mithanraj S, Dinesh Kumar V
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: The rising prevalence of melanoma and nonmelanoma conditions has made skin-related malignancies one of today's most pressing healthcare challenges. During diagnosis, dermoscopic image analysis is often hindered by hair and shadows that obscure critical lesion details. We present a Variational Autoencoder-Generative Adversarial Network (VAE-GAN) framework enhanced with the Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) for effective hair removal in dermoscopic images. The proposed encoderdecoder framework integrates DWT-based multiresolution analysis to effectively capture and distinguish authentic lesion textures from reconstructed components using adversarial learning. This approach improves the visual quality of dermoscopic images while retaining important fine-grained structural information. The training process is guided by a combined loss function consisting of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{L 1}$</tex> loss, total variation loss, and structural similarity (SSIM), which helps in achieving precise pixel reconstruction and maintaining structural consistency. For experimental validation, 5,000 dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset were used for both training and evaluation.

title: Generative Adversarial Network Assisted Feature Augmentation for Credit Scoring Enhancement
authors: Krishna Chaitanya Yarlagadda
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Credit scores are crucial for financial decisionmaking as they help organisations to estimate the ability of borrowers to repay their debts. Class imbalance, missing patterns, properties of input data often make traditional models such as logistic regression and support vector machines perform poorly. These limitations make it difficult to identify complex patterns in debtor behaviour and reduce the predictability of the model. To address this problem, a framework for augmenting features, supported by generative adversarial networks (GANs), is proposed, which generate artificial but meaningful features that improve the original dataset. Better feature diversity and better ability to differentiate between creditworthy and noncreditworthy applicants are achieved when the discriminator verifies the authenticity of new feature representations generated by the generator. Then, a credit score classifier is then trained on both the augmented features and the original dataset using gradient enhancement and the decision boundary is reinforced also hidden relationships are captured by this process. The area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUCROC), accuracy, recall, F1 score, precision are used to evaluate the proposed work, and the metrics show how well the implementation is able to balances stability across algorithms, accurate positive predictions, detection of default risk, According to the tested results, GAN-assisted augmentation is improved to conventional methodologies for the function and provides significant improvements in all metrics.

title: Differential Performance and Contextual Integrity in Speech Privacy
authors: Poppy Welch, Natalia Tomashenko, Sneha Das, Jennifer Williams
publish: IEEE Security & Privacy
abstract: Speech technology challenges traditional privacy frameworks due to the complexity of speech and diverse contextual norms. We apply contextual integrity to three speech case studies, showing its promise for speech privacy and recommending preliminary assessments of signal, speaker, and system characterizations.

title: Confidence-Aware Test-Time Adaptation for Fault-Resilient Sound Source Localization Guided by Lightweight Diagnosis
authors: Inkyu An
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Deep learning-based sound source localization systems typically rely on specific microphone array configurations, making them highly vulnerable to hardware failures. Unexpected sensor faults cause severe structural signal degradation, leading to substantial deterioration in localization accuracy. To address this challenge, we propose a robust Fault-Resilient Sound Source Localization framework that autonomously diagnoses sensor faults and adapts the model at test time without requiring ground-truth labels. The proposed system integrates two core modules: 1) a Lightweight Fault Diagnosis module, which employs a computationally efficient Frequency-Time Projection Network with a Fault Queue mechanism, and 2) a Diagnosis-Guided Confidence-Aware Test-Time Adaptation strategy, which leverages the highly reliable diagnosis results to apply interventions and utilizes a confidence thresholding mechanism to generate refined hard pseudo-labels for stable self-training. As a result, the proposed system reduces the mean absolute error, averaged across diverse models and fault conditions, from 21.99° to 9.91°, achieving a significant error reduction of 54.92%, thereby ensuring reliable robotic perception even in the presence of sudden hardware malfunctions. The proposed framework is further validated in a real-environment experiment using a physical microphone array with an authentic hardware fault, confirming its generalizability beyond synthetic fault conditions.

title: Economic Impacts and Mitigation for the Grid Connected Electric Vehicle Charging: A Technical and Operational Overview
authors: Oyakhilome Ereme Ikhajangbe, Himavarsha Dhulipati, Shichao Liu
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The rapid growth of residential electric vehicle (EV) adoption is reshaping distribution networks, shifting load patterns from relatively dispersed background demand to synchronized, high-amplitude charging events. When charging is unmanaged, these concentrated loads can exceed thermal and voltage limits, accelerate transformer aging, intensify harmonic stress, and prompt costly infrastructure upgrades. Unlike prior reviews that examine technical, economic, and coordination aspects separately, this paper brings them together within an integrated technical–economic analytical review that considers thermal limits, voltage security, and battery state-of-charge constraints simultaneously. Transformer loss-of-life, harmonic–thermal interactions, peak amplification, and renewable–EV timing mismatch are quantitatively analyzed alongside infrastructure deferral benefits and ancillary-service revenues. Economic performance is evaluated by balancing upgrade deferral, V2G revenue, battery degradation costs, and coordination overhead. A comparative assessment of ARIMA, LSTM, and reinforcement learning methods highlights practical trade-offs between forecasting accuracy and computational scalability under evolving charging behavior. Evidence from field pilots reports 10–30% peak reduction, 20–65% charging cost savings, and $100–$500 per EV-year gross V2G revenue, depending on market structure and incentives. Overall, this review provides an integrated analytical foundation to support resilient and economically efficient EV–grid integration under high-penetration conditions.

title: Bayesian Deep-Learning Processor for Real-Time Bio-Applications With Structured Monte Carlo Dropout for High-Volume Sample Generation
authors: Han-Gyeol Mun, Jeong-Min Woo, Seunghyun Moon, Byungjun Kim, Jongmin Lee, Jae-Yoon Sim, Hyunwoo Son
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: This work presents a Bayesian neural network (BNN) processor for real-time, edge-based medical applications, designed to generate high-volume inference outputs efficiently, providing rapid and precise uncertainty estimation. To address the computational and memory-intensive demands of BNNs, we propose a structured Monte Carlo (MC) dropout method and an efficient processing technique for depth-wise separable convolution, thereby minimizing both the memory and computational burdens. The proposed processor, implemented in 28-nm LP CMOS, is benchmarked on a 12-lead ECG dataset and demonstrates significant improvements in energy efficiency, achieving a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$12.9\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> enhancement over conventional MC dropout methods. Furthermore, the processor incorporates a model-switching technique based on uncertainty estimation, resulting in <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$3.2\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> lower energy consumption while maintaining robust and accurate ECG classification, even under challenging conditions involving noise and motion artifacts.

title: Learning to Generate Preferences for Multiobjective Deep Learning
authors: Peixin Huang, Yu Sun, Gang Wang, Yaoxin Wu, Qiong Wu, Wen Song, Yew-Soon Ong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: Multiobjective optimization (MOO) is important for deep learning applications with multiple conflicting objectives. Pareto front learning (PFL) methods learn a single model conditioned on the preference of objectives and can be applied to any preference at inference time. However, existing PFL methods use predefined strategies (e.g., uniform sampling) to generate preferences, which could result in unevenly spaced solutions since the shape of Pareto front is largely ignored. In this article, we propose a lightweight and model-agnostic method to train a preference generator for a given PFL model, which learns to generate proper preferences from uniformly sampled ones, such that the resulting solutions are evenly spaced on the Pareto front. Compared to previous works, our method enables a more rational allocation of preferences, which can either be utilized to enhance a pretrained PFL model or be seamlessly integrated into the PFL training process to improve efficiency. We apply our method to state-of-the-art PFL methods with various backbones (e.g., multilayer perceptron, convolutional neural network, transformer) and validate the significance of preference generation across various tasks, from multitask supervised learning to multiobjective reinforcement learning-based neural combinatorial optimization. Experimental results show that our method improves the backbone algorithm in most settings, showing its effectiveness and general applicability.

title: Hybrid Noise and Jamming Modulation: An Efficient Anti-jamming Perspective
authors: Yuxin Shi, Xinjin Lu, Yusi Zhang, Cheng Li, Chen Han, Fanggang Wang, Symeon Chatzinotas
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: Recently, the noise modulation scheme and active anti-jamming (AAJ) scheme have shown the potential advantages, such as high anti-jamming performance, low transmitter complexity and low power consumption. Inspired by the prior schemes, this paper proposes a novel hybrid noise and jamming modulation (NJM) scheme that enables reliable communication under diverse jamming scenarios. Specifically, this scheme encodes information bits through a noise modulated transmitter and the amplification factor of a programmable gain amplifier (PGA), achieving robust anti-jamming performance. Theoretical bit error rate (BER) is conducted to derive the optimal decoding thresholds for different jamming strategies, and the approximate optimal threshold proposed for practical implementation using training symbols. Furthermore, we formulate and solve the power allocation optimization problem to enhance BER performance by properly splitting power between the two modulation components. To comprehensively evaluate the scheme, we provide the capacity analysis for the hybrid NJM scheme under three jamming strategies. Simulation results show that the theoretical results match well with the simulated ones, which demonstrates the correctness of our BER performance analysis. Moreover, the hybrid NJM scheme can achieve the superior BER performance and the channel capacity under the jamming attacks compared with the benchmark schemes.

title: Theoretical Gain-Bandwidth Limit of Parametrically Tuned Electrically Small Receiving Antennas
authors: C. G. Blosser, K. Schab, K. C. Kerby-Patel, J. E. Ruyle
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: Theoretical limits on the gain and bandwidth of a class of receiving, electrically small antennas (ESAs) loaded with periodically time-varying (TV) reactances is analyzed. The TV reactance produces parametric amplification and its average reactance tunes the ESA to resonance. A multi-band circuit model is developed that is suitable for designing these ESAs. The model is analyzed using conversion matrix techniques to obtain expressions for maximum transducer gain and half-power bandwidth. The trade-off between maximum gain and bandwidth is analyzed, and the circuit parameter values required to approach the maximum gain for a fixed bandwidth are determined.

title: Efficient Incremental Updates for Encrypted Mobile Apps
authors: Lewei Jin, Kui Zhang, Tong Sun, Jiamei Lv, Yi Gao, Wei Dong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing
abstract: The growth in mobile applications and user numbers has significantly increased the volume of app updates on the network. For example, TikTok has garnered 825 million downloads in 2024 and maintains a nearly weekly update frequency. To reduce the data traffic, incremental update has become a common solution, as it calculates and transmits only the differences between two versions of an app. However, the widespread adoption of application encryption has introduced new challenges for efficient incremental updates. Even minor changes in files or encryption keys can result in substantial differences between app versions that were initially highly similar. This paper presents EncDiffPatch, the first incremental update system tailored for encrypted mobile apps. EncDiffPatch leverages two key techniques to enable efficient updates. First, it uses an updating-aware key sharing mechanism, which utilizes file-level encryption and dynamically shares the encryption keys for files that remain unchanged across versions based on the content of these files. Second, it incorporates the decryption process with a selective decompression approach. This approach performs only the Huffman decoding process of decompression and decompresses only those bytes estimated to contribute to differencing benefits. Evaluation shows that EncDiffPatch achieves a 51% reduction in compression ratio without introducing any performance overhead. When incorporating the decryption process, EncDiffPatch achieves a 73% reduction in compression ratio, outperforming the state-of the-art algorithms while requiring only 45.3% of the reconstruction time. Our security analysis shows that EncDiffPatch maintains an equivalent level of security to existing mobile systems.

title: Evaluating the Impact of Jitter on Collaborative High-Speed Aerial and Railway Networks
authors: Ziyue Liu, Yue Xiao, Enzhi Zhou, Shuting Chen, Xianfu Lei, Xingwang Li, Sotiris A. Tegos, Panagiotis D. Diamantoulakis, George K. Karagiannidis
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: With the rapid growth of high-speed rail (HSR) networks, reliable communication is increasingly challenging due to complex terrain and coverage gaps in remote areas. Uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs), with their mobility and flexibility, provide aerial relay support to bridge these gaps, enhance signal strength, and improve HSR communication reliability. However, their performance in millimeter-wave (mmWave) systems is significantly degraded by mechanical jitter caused by environmental factors such as wind and turbulence, which adversely affects beam alignment and overall communication quality. To address these challenges, this work introduces a comprehensive analytical framework. We first develop a statistical model that characterizes the relationship between beam gain and jitter intensity. Subsequently, closed-form expressions are derived for the outage probability and ergodic data rate of UAV-assisted HSR mmWave systems under jitter influence, taking into account both co-located (CA) and distributed antenna (DA) configurations. Furthermore, we propose an adaptive beamwidth design that maximizes the average ergodic rate by adjusting the beamwidth according to UAV jitter severity. Numerical simulations verify that this approach significantly improves system capacity and robustness compared with conventional static beamforming, confirming its effectiveness.

title: A Method for Slip Angle Estimation of Intelligent Tire System Based on Hierarchical Temporal Fusion Network
authors: Jinghan Huang, Xiaolong Zhang, Chao Liu, Licheng Yan
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Accurately estimating the slip angle under dynamic conditions is critical for enhancing vehicle stability and safety. Intelligent tire systems and data-driven deep learning methods have been adopted to slip angle estimation. However, existing approaches often suffer from inadequate temporal feature extraction and limited model–data compatibility. To address these limitations, we perform a temporal dependency analysis of tire acceleration data, revealing strong local and global correlations. Based on this, we propose a novel feature extraction algorithm and a Hierarchical Temporal Fusion Network to fully capture these multiscale temporal correlations. Additionally, an Intelligent Linkage mechanism is designed to enhance model convergence speed and generalization capabilities. Linear probing and principal component analysis further confirm that the network learns highly discriminative and physically continuous representations. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves 99.38% prediction accuracy with only 11,842 parameters and processes more than 96,000 samples/s, outperforming baseline networks in both accuracy and efficiency. On-road validation confirms its applicability, reaching over 92% agreement on both dry and wet pavements against a kinematic reference.

title: Development of an SRAM Neutron Detector for Pulsed Fusion Experiments
authors: Wolfgang Hennig, Nate Kaneshige, Sara Alawabdeh, Dan Cech, Michael Hua
publish: IEEE Transactions on Plasma Science
abstract: Radiation is known to cause single-event upsets in semiconductor devices, which leads to data errors in SRAM memory (bitflips). While usually undesirable, this effect can also be used as a mechanism to detect radiation, in particular neutrons. Previous research shows that SRAM memory is capable of detecting neutrons without permanent damage to the device, and that devices from different processes and/or manufacturers vary in properties such as neutron interaction energy threshold and overall cross section. In the work described here, an SRAM bit flip neutron detector was assembled, programmed, and tested with a variety of sources. Two SRAM types with identical chip package and functionality, from Renesas and Integrated Silicon Solution Inc. (ISSI), were tested with a Cf-252 source, deuterium-deuterium (DD) and deuterium-tritium (DT) neutron generators, and high-yield neutron pulses from the Godiva (fission) and Z-pinch experimental underground system (ZEUS) (DT fusion) facilities. Additional tests were performed with decapsulated chips and an alpha source. We observe that the number of bitflips increases linearly with the neutron fluence. The ISSI SRAM is physically a smaller die, but shows <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula>–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$100\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> more bit flips per neutron than the Renesas SRAM, depending on the neutron source. The bitflips are distributed randomly across memory addresses, except for groups of three close neighbors observed in the Renesas SRAM. No damage to the SRAM has been observed, even in the intense EMI and high instantaneous flux of the ZEUS pulsed neutron fields. With a suitable calibration for bitflips per incident neutrons, the detector can be used for quantitative measurements, and there is potential to use the ISSI versus Renesas type to differentiate between DT and DD neutrons. With a cost of <U.S. 10 per memory chip with four million bits, the design is highly scalable into large arrays for higher sensitivity. The chips are also conducive to rapid replacement in the case of operational damage. On Helion Energy’s Polaris fusion generator, the SRAM detector will be fielded as a relative yield neutron fluence detector with regular calibrations against indium and zirconium neutron activation foils.

title: An Enhanced Multiple Intrusion Event Localization Algorithm Based on Dual MZ-Sagnac Optical Fiber Sensing Perimeter Security System
authors: Yang Yan, Churui Li, Rui Jin, Chenyue He, Yuzhe Sun, Yunke Du, Chao Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Interferometric distributed optical fiber vibration sensing technology has been widely applied in the field of perimeter security. Among various structures, the dual MZ–Sagnac optical fiber sensing perimeter security system (the dual MZ–Sagnac system) plays an important role in perimeter security due to its high sensitivity, electromagnetic resistance, corrosion resistance, high stability and low cost. However, under the condition of low-SNR signals, the traditional event localization method for the dual MZ–Sagnac system, which directly averages the localization results of continuous short-interval signals, leads to great errors. In this paper, by employing the Cram´er-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) theory of cross-correlation time delay estimation method, a Weighted Gaussian Superposition (WGS) algorithm based on the dual MZ–Sagnac system is proposed to enhance event localization accuracy and distinguish multiple vibration events. Vibration signals are collected and CRLB conformity to the dual MZ–Sagnac system is validated. Furthermore, the WGS algorithm reveals a minimum MSE of 1.63, corresponding to the error of 1.28 m and an accuracy improvement of 72 % in the experiment. Additionally, an application is performed to evaluate the effectiveness of the WGS algorithm for multiple events discrimination and localization. In perimeter security scenarios utilizing the dual MZ-Sagnac system, the WGS algorithm not only further refines the localization precision but also enables the system with multiple events discrimination, allowing the measurement instrumentation to better adapt to engineering applications.

title: Improving Out-of-Distribution Generalization on Point Clouds with Cross-Domain Adversarial Distillation
authors: Bowei Xing, Xianghua Ying, Ruibin Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Point cloud is an important data format to represent 3D objects and real world scenes, which is widely used in many fields such as virtual reality and autonomous driving. In recent years, various methods have been proposed for point cloud recognition and achieved great performance. However, since these methods are always under the i.i.d. assumption, the trained model inevitably suffers drastic performance degradation when evaluated on out-of-distribution (OOD) data. To address the above problem, in this paper, we propose a framework named cross-domain adversarial distillation, with the goal of learning a model that generalizes well on OOD point clouds. Concretely, we initiate by dynamically generating adversarial point cloud examples that deviate from original domain and exhibit diversification, so as to progressively broaden the training distribution. Subsequently, we implement a bidirectional distillation scheme to ensure semantic consistency between the generated novel-domain example and source domain example, which facilitates the exchange of cross-domain knowledge through teacher-student cooperation. By iteratively alternating between the above processes, the model is encouraged to learn more generalizable representations when encountering point clouds from novel distributions, leading to improved performance on the unseen target domain. We carry experiments on two typical datasets, ModelNet40-C for corrupted point clouds and Sim-to-Real for real-world point clouds, implemented with different 3D recognition models. The experimental results show that our method achieves better performance on these two benchmarks, which demonstrate the effectiveness of our method for improving point cloud OOD generalization.

title: Vulnerabilities in AI-Generated Image Detection: The Challenge of Adversarial Attacks
authors: Yunfeng Diao, Naixin Zhai, Changtao Miao, Zitong Yu, Xingxing Wei, Xun Yang, Meng Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Recent advancements in image synthesis, particularly with the advent of GAN and Diffusion models, have amplified public concerns regarding the dissemination of disinformation. To address such concerns, numerous AI-generated Image (AIGI) Detectors have been proposed and achieved promising performance in identifying fake images. However, there still lacks a systematic understanding of the adversarial robustness of AIGI detectors. In this paper, we examine the vulnerability of state-of-the-art AIGI detectors against adversarial attack under white-box and black-box settings, which has been rarely investigated so far. To this end, we propose a new method to attack AIGI detectors. First, inspired by the obvious difference between real images and fake images in the frequency domain, we add perturbations under the frequency domain to push the image away from its original frequency distribution. Second, we explore the full posterior distribution of the surrogate model to further narrow this gap between heterogeneous AIGI detectors, e.g., transferring adversarial examples across CNNs and ViTs. This is achieved by introducing a novel post-train Bayesian strategy that turns a single surrogate into a Bayesian one, capable of simulating diverse victim models using one pre-trained surrogate, without the need for re-training. We name our method as Frequency-based Post-train Bayesian Attack, or FPBA. Through FPBA, we demonstrate that adversarial attacks pose a real threat to AIGI detectors. FPBA can deliver successful black-box attacks across various detectors, generators, defense methods, and even evade cross-generator and compressed image detection, which are crucial real-world detection scenarios. Our code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/onotoa/fpba</uri>.

title: GeneralizGeneralizing Beyond Patterns: Dynamic Moment Query Recalibrating for Out-of-Distribution Video Temporal Localizationing
authors: Zhuo Tao, Liang Li, Yunbin Tu, Jiong Yin, Beichen Zhang, Zheng-Jun Zha, Qingming Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Video temporal localization (VTL) aims to accurately identify the temporal boundary of a specific segment corresponding to a sentence query from an untrimmed video. Recently, DETR-based methods have emerged as effective solutions to VTL. The primary idea of these methods is to fuse multi-modal features and refine moment queries to obtain precise locations. Despite their success, these methods adopt fixed trainable moment queries, which implicitly model the statistical patterns of groundtruth segments in the training set and thus limit the model's generalization ability to out-of-distribution scenarios. To tackle this problem, we propose a Dynamic moment Query Recalibrated DEtection TRansformer (DQR-DETR) that adaptively generates and recalibrates multi-scale moment queries on the target segments for out-of-distribution VTL. Our method contains three key parts: (1) progressive semantic regularization that performs primitive modality consensus loss and refined semantic calibration loss at two phases for better global and fine-grained cross-modal associations; (2) hierarchical query decoupling that first dynamically extracts multi-scale and context-aware event queries from the joint visual-language representations to cover a wide range of potential events in video, and then select the sentence-aware queries with semantic guidance; (3) moment query recalibrating that performs coarse-to-fine modulation on multi-scale queries by first activating relevant queries with video highlight and sentence semantic guidance, and then refining them to focus on the target segments. Extensive experiments on QVHighlights, Charades-STA and TACoS datasets show that our DQR-DETR achieves state-of-the-art performance, especially in out-of-distribution scenarios where our method substantially outperforms existing approaches by large margins.

title: Dual Representation Aggregation Network for Blind Image Super-Resolution via Iterative Bi-level Optimization
authors: Xianhong Wen, Sheng Ren, Bin Hu, Xiangyuan Zhu, Zehui Xiao, Kehua Guo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Blind image super-resolution (BSR) aims to reconstruct high-resolution (HR) images from low-resolution (LR) images with unknown degradations. Existing BSR methods mainly follow two pipelines: LR prior-based reconstruction and SR posterior-based reconstruction. However, LR prior-based methods are sensitive to degradation and lack correction, and SR posterior-based approaches face limitations in generalization. From the perspective of aggregating both LR priors and SR posteriors, we propose a multi-branch network, Dual Representation Aggregation Network (DRANet) that constructs degradation aware branch and deviation correction branch hierarchically. Moreover, the end-to-end case reveals that the interaction between two branches poses a significant challenge, leading to model collapse and poor performance. To address this issue, we propose Iterative Bi-level Optimization (IBO), which decouples the DRANet learning problem into three sub-problems for staged training. We first optimize the parameters of two branches separately. Once each branch converges to a satisfactory performance, we conduct joint training through an iterative approach. Experiments on several blind degradation settings show that our DRANet produces promising results compared to state-of-the-art BSR methods on various benchmarks. Ablation studies indicate the effectiveness of dual representation aggregation and the stability of IBO, compared to both single and end-to-end strategies. The code is available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/para999/IBO</uri>.

title: Practical and Robust Privacy-Preserving K-Means Clustering Protocols With GPU Acceleration
authors: Tao Bai, Yuanqing Feng, Zhi Lu, Xueming Tang, Songfeng Lu, Yongquan Cui
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computing
abstract: Privacy-preserving machine learning allows for the protection of the original data's privacy while training models. In recent years, researchers have implemented machine learning algorithms using secure multi-party computation (MPC) techniques, with clustering being one of the key applications. However, current privacy-preserving clustering algorithms still face challenges regarding execution speed and communication overhead. In this paper, we propose a new three-party GPU-accelerated privacy-preserving K-means clustering algorithm aimed at improving computational efficiency in distributed settings. We design a three-party K-means algorithm and optimize subprotocols such as the truncation protocol and the constant-round comparison protocol to reduce communication latency. Additionally, we leverage the powerful capabilities of Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) to enhance the execution efficiency of the algorithm, particularly for intensive distance computation and centroid updates. Finally, we propose a privacy-preserving clustering quality evaluation protocol to address the parameter selection issue (determining the optimal number of clusters $K$) in privacy-preserving K-means. Experimental results on the synthetic datasets demonstrate that our method achieves a $137\times$–$221\times$ improvement in efficiency compared to the CPU-based protocol of Mohassel et al., and a $5.7\times$–$11.2\times$ speedup over the CPU-based state-of-the-art implementation by Wei et al..

title: An Enhanced Elliptically-Deformed Folded Waveguide for E-Band Traveling Wave Tubes with Hot-Test Validation
authors: Han Lai, Fujun Xiao, Zhihao Xing, Mengjie Yuan, Zhaoyun Duan, Yubin Gong, Dun Lu, Huarong Gong
publish: IEEE Electron Device Letters
abstract: This letter presents a novel elliptically-deformed folded waveguide (ED-FWG) with enhanced electromagnetic characteristics, holding promise for high-performance traveling wave tubes (TWTs) in future broadband wireless communication and radar systems. Compared to the conventional structure, the proposed ED-FWG exhibits improved interaction impedance and higher circuit gain per unit length, thereby facilitating device miniaturization. Meanwhile, the ED-FWG exhibits both flat dispersion and high reflectivity, contributing to a broader bandwidth. It provides greater design flexibility and involves no additional fabrication complexity. An E-band prototype based on this structure was fabricated and tested. The device achieves a saturated output power exceeding 130 W and a peak power of 170 W in the 71-76 GHz band. The gain exceeds 52.5 dB, reaching a maximum of 56.2 dB. These results validate the ED-FWG as an effective solution for enhancing TWTs. Its design principles could be applied to other higher-frequency bands, especially those that demand high power, high gain, and compact power sources.

title: A Communication Failure and Cyber-Attack Resilient Wide-Area Out-of-Step Prediction Scheme Using Dynamic State Estimation
authors: Amir Dadashi, Shahin Riahinia, Amir Ameli, Hassan Naser, Mohsen Ghafouri
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Systems
abstract: Out-of-step (OOS) prediction is essential for reducing mechanical and electrical stress on power system components. A reliable strategy involves using wide-area measurement systems (WAMS) to monitor the dynamic behavior of voltage angle differences across transmission lines and generator rotor angles. However, the limited availability of phasor measurement units (PMUs) presents a challenge, as voltage angle measurements are not available at all buses. Moreover, rotor angles cannot be directly measured and must be estimated. This paper proposes an OOS prediction scheme based on dynamic state estimation (DSE), which concurrently estimates both algebraic and dynamic states. By calculating the slip frequency and the acceleration of voltage angle differences, the electrical center's location is predicted by tracking its trajectory on the slip-acceleration plane. A similar approach is used to predict generator OOS based on the estimated rotor angles. Since the scheme depends on WAMS data, it is susceptible to cyber-threats such as false data injection attacks (FDIAs), denial-of-service attacks (DoSAs), and communication failures. To improve resilience, the proposed method integrates a penalized weighted least squares (PWLS) approach into an extended Kalman filter (EKF) for detecting and mitigating attacks and handling data loss. The scheme is validated on the IEEE 39-bus test system under various network topologies and attack scenarios.

title: Subarray-based Phase-less Angular Parameter Estimation for an Incoherently Distributed Source
authors: Zhe Li, Mingkang Hou, Ziyu Guo, Bo Hu
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: This letter proposes to leverage the subarray-based antenna array structure to estimate the angular parameters of an incoherently distributed source. It only needs to observe the received power and does not need the phase of the subarrays, where the number of antenna elements is set to increase by one across adjacent subarrays. An approximate expression for the second-order difference of the received power is derived first, assuming that the source has a symmetric distribution. Based on that, the nominal angle-of-arrival can be estimated without knowing the distribution. If the distribution is given, the angular spread can be estimated directly. Compared with the methods developed on fully digital arrays, the proposed method has much lower computational complexity. Meanwhile, the simulation results show that it can achieve higher angular spread estimation accuracy.

title: SwarmRouter: Obstacle-Avoiding Routing for Droplet Swarms on Active-Matrix Digital Microfluidic Biochips
authors: Ziyang Chen, Zhiwen Yu, Haoyang Xu, Bin Guo, Chunyu Chang, Sizhao Li, Tsung-Yi Ho, Hanbin Ma, Xing Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Digital microfluidic biochips (DMFBs), renowned for their programmable and digital manipulations of droplets, have been extensively applied in the automation of bioassays. However, constrained by factors such as electrode size, quantity, and addressing methods, conventional DMFBs face challenges in deploying large-scale biochemical applications. In contrast, the next-generation active-matrix digital microfluidic biochips (AM-DMFs), leveraging their powerful capabilities in cross-addressing and parallel droplet manipulations, have emerged as a key alternative platform for high-throughput bioassays. Nevertheless, the execution of large-scale bioassays on the AM-DMF platforms poses significant challenges for droplet routing. For example, in single-cell proteomics assays, to facilitate high-throughput processing of numerous samples, hundreds and thousands of split droplets need to be transported in parallel to designated areas while avoiding on-chip obstacles. This gives rise to the large-scale obstacle-avoiding routing problem, which existing approaches prove inadequate to effectively address. In this paper, we propose the first obstacle-avoiding routing method called SwarmRouter for large-scale droplet swarms, which enables efficient path planning for droplets while ensuring conflict-free and deadlock-free operations. The proposed method includes the following key techniques: 1) A geometric-temporal coupled preprocessing strategy integrating droplet spacing optimization and priority scheduling for conflict and deadlock elimination, 2) a path preselection strategy for both path length reduction and conflict avoidance, and 3) a timing-driven parallel routing method with dynamic checkpoints for waiting time minimization. Experimental results on an ACXEL AM16K biochip system demonstrate that SwarmRouter successfully realizes conflict-free path planning for all given droplets, while achieving significant reductions of 25.13% in total path length, 19.6% in maximum arrival time, and an improvement of 12.63% in average droplet velocity compared to a baseline method.

title: Co-Divide: Multi-Granularity Consistency Learning for Remote Sensing Image Retrieval under Severe Noisy Labels
authors: Yeyun Wu, Xiaolin Gao, Junsheng Wang, Tiantian Gong
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Cross-domain content-based remote sensing image retrieval (CBRSIR) under noisy environments represents a promising yet challenging research direction, focusing on knowledge transfer from noise-corrupted source domain to unlabeled target domain. However, existing domain adaptation methods often overlook the characteristic of remote sensing images (RSIs) exhibiting large intra-class variance and small inter-class variance, failing to meet the requirements adequately. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework called CDMCL (co-divide and multi-granularity consistency learning). First, we introduce a dual-model collaborative divide (co-divide) and correction (co-correction) strategy, where two heterogeneous Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) interactively separate clean samples and rectify noisy labels via neighbor consistency. Second, we innovatively employ a graph neural network (GNN) module to extract domain-invariant features. Finally, we design a strongly augmented and weakly augmented consistency learning strategy in the target domain, combining maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) and prototype constraints to achieve multi-granularity feature alignment. Additionally, a contrastive learning mechanism is introduced to further enhance the discriminative capability of target domain features. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CDMCL significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods on multiple cross-domain remote sensing retrieval benchmarks. In particular, CDMCL achieves a 19.98% performance improvement on the DB2 dataset under a severe noisy scenario with 60% noisy labels.

title: Lightweight YOLO Architectures for Efficient Dog Emotion Classification
authors: Viencentius Delvin Frelim, Martin Sebastian, Auryn Larissa Sabrina, Samuel Philip
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Blockchain, Cloud Computing, and Data Analytics (ICoABCD)
abstract: This study investigates the effectiveness of lightweight YOLO based classification architectures, namely YOLOv8s cls and YOLOv11s cls, for automatic dog emotion recognition. The emotions angry, happy, relaxed, and sad are defined according to the Circumplex Model of Affect. A curated dataset consisting of 3,600 images, with 900 images per class, was constructed and preprocessed through quality filtering, duplicate removal, and feature based clustering to ensure data diversity and class balance. To guarantee a fair comparison, both models were trained using identical preprocessing pipelines, image resolutions, learning rates, batch sizes, epoch numbers, and evaluation metrics. Model training and validation were conducted using five fold cross validation, while final performance was evaluated on a separate test set excluded from training and validation. Experimental results show that YOLOv8s cls achieved the best performance, with an accuracy of 86.42 percent and a macro average <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$F 1$</tex> score of 86.43 percent, surpassing YOLOv11s cls, which obtained 85.28 percent accuracy and an F1 score of 85.22 percent. Both YOLO based models also outperformed conventional convolutional neural network architectures such as VGG16 and ResNet50 reported previously. These findings highlight the potential of lightweight YOLO classifiers as fast, accurate, and resource efficient solutions for real time dog emotion recognition.

title: Spatiotemporal Integrated Optimization of Intersection with Inner-Widened Short Lanes
authors: Mengru Liu
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Computing (SPIC)
abstract: Short lanes are commonly adopted when physical constraints render full lane expansion infeasible at intersections. This study proposes a spatio-temporal integrated intersection design model featuring inner widened short lanes - a design that converts exit lanes into entry lanes through strategic innermost lane widening. Each additional lane requiring 3.0-3.2 meters of width. The proposed model incorporates both number and allocation of short lanes as decision variables into the conventional lane-based model, overcoming the limitations of existing research confined to fixed short lane number and predetermined lane allocation. Accordingly, choosing intersection capacity as the objective, we formulate the design model as a binary mixed-integer nonlinear programming (BMINLP) problem that simultaneously determines the optimal cycle length, timing plan, and lane allocation. Results show that when short lane capacity reaches 10 vehicles, implementing inner widened short lanes increases intersection capacity by 21% compared to conventional designs. When capacity exceeds the threshold of 4 vehicles, the optimal cycle length no longer adopts the maximum value and shows positive correlation with storage capacity. These findings provide theoretical basis for intersection design optimization and establish practical benchmarks for validating data-driven control strategies in intelligent transportation systems.

title: Preface to the Special Issue
authors: Danial Faghihi, Prashant K. Jha, Serge Prudhomme
publish: Computing in Science & Engineering
abstract: This special issue is meant to celebrate the lifetime achievements of J. Tinsley Oden (1936– 2023) in the course of his career. He is recognized by many as a pioneer in the field of computational mechanics, a term he coined himself early on, and as the founder of the Oden Institute for Computational Engineering and Sciences at The University of Texas at Austin.

title: Average-Case Benchmarking of Optimizers With Environmental Impact Metrics
authors: Jamel Raddaoui, Yassine Ben Salem, Walid Mechri
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: This paper addresses an extensive empirical analysis of Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models, trained systematically on diverse benchmark datasets for image classification using a broad spectrum of optimization techniques. For each distinct model-dataset-optimizer triplet, we rigorously record a multidimensional suite of performance indicators, encompassing accuracy, training time, energy consumption, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CO}_{2}$</tex> emissions, F1-score, precision and recall. The full set of performance measures was averaged to ensure stability. This study not only identifies the most effective optimizer for each architectural and dataset configuration but also offers a comparative analysis of the overall optimization efficiency and learning performance, providing actionable insights into the trade-offs between accuracy and resource efficiency in deep learning model training.
